 item_def_id: I::DefId,\n   145→        a_arg: I::GenericArgs,\n   146→        b_arg: I::GenericArgs,\n   147→    ) -> RelateResult<I, I::GenericArgs> {\n   148→        if self.ambient_variance == ty::Invariant {\n   149→            // Avoid fetching the variance if we are in an invariant\n   150→            // context; no need, and it can induce dependency cycles\n   151→            // (e.g., #41849).\n   152→            relate_args_invariantly(self, a_arg, b_arg)\n   153→        } else {\n   154→            let tcx = self.cx();\n   155→            let opt_variances = tcx.variances_of(item_def_id);\n   156→            relate_args_with_variances(self, item_def_id, opt_variances, a_arg, b_arg, false)\n   157→        }\n   158→    }\n   159→\n   160→    fn relate_with_variance<T: Relate<I>>(\n   161→        &mut self,\n   162→        variance: ty::Variance,\n   163→        _info: VarianceDiagInfo<I>,\n   164→        a: T,\n   165→        b: T,\n   166→    ) -> RelateResult<I, T> {\n   167→        let old_ambient_variance = self.ambient_variance;\n   168→        self.ambient_variance = self.ambient_variance.xform(variance);\n   169→        debug!(?self.ambient_variance, \"new ambient variance\");\n   170→\n   171→        let r = if self.ambient_variance == ty::Bivariant { Ok(a) } else { self.relate(a, b) };\n   172→\n   173→        self.ambient_variance = old_ambient_variance;\n   174→        r\n   175→    }\n   176→\n   177→    #[instrument(skip(self), level = \"trace\")]\n   178→    fn tys(&mut self, a: I::Ty, b: I::Ty) -> RelateResult<I, I::Ty> {\n   179→        if a == b {\n   180→            return Ok(a);\n   181→        }\n   182→\n   183→        let infcx = self.infcx;\n   184→        let a = infcx.shallow_resolve(a);\n   185→        let b = infcx.shallow_resolve(b);\n   186→\n   187→        if self.cache.contains(&(self.ambient_variance, a, b)) {\n   188→            return Ok(a);\n   189→        }\n   190→\n   191→        match (a.kind(), b.kind()) {\n   192→            (ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(a_id)), ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(b_id))) => {\n   193→                match self.ambient_variance {\n   194→                    ty::Covariant => {\n   195→                        // can't make progress on `A <: B` if both A and B are\n   196→                        // type variables, so record an obligation.\n   197→                        self.goals.push(Goal::new(\n   198→                            self.cx(),\n   199→                            self.param_env,\n   200→                            ty::Binder::dummy(ty::PredicateKind::Subtype(ty::SubtypePredicate {\n   201→                                a_is_expected: true,\n   202→                                a,\n   203→                                b,\n   204→                            })),\n   205→                        ));\n   206→                    }\n   207→                    ty::Contravariant => {\n   208→                        // can't make progress on `B <: A` if both A and B are\n   209→                        // type variables, so record an obligation.\n   210→                        self.goals.push(Goal::new(\n   211→                            self.cx(),\n   212→                            self.param_env,\n   213→                            ty::Binder::dummy(ty::PredicateKind::Subtype(ty::SubtypePredicate {\n   214→                                a_is_expected: false,\n   215→                                a: b,\n   216→                                b: a,\n   217→                            })),\n   218→                        ));\n   219→                    }\n   220→                    ty::Invariant => {\n   221→                        infcx.equate_ty_vids_raw(a_id, b_id);\n   222→                    }\n   223→                    ty::Bivariant => {\n   224→                        unreachable!(\"Expected bivariance to be handled in relate_with_variance\")\n   225→                    }\n   226→                }\n   227→            }\n   228→\n   229→            (ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(a_vid)), _) => {\n   230→                infcx.instantiate_ty_var_raw(self, true, a_vid, self.ambient_variance, b)?;\n   231→            }\n   232→            (_, ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(b_vid))) => {\n   233→                infcx.instantiate_ty_var_raw(\n   234→                    self,\n   235→                    false,\n   236→                    b_vid,\n   237→                    self.ambient_variance.xform(ty::Contravariant),\n   238→                    a,\n   239→                )?;\n   240→            }\n   241→\n   242→            _ => {\n   243→                super_combine_tys(self.infcx, self, a, b)?;\n   244→            }\n   245→        }\n   246→\n   247→        assert!(self.cache.insert((self.ambient_variance, a, b)));\n   248→\n   249→        Ok(a)\n   250→    }\n   251→\n   252→    #[instrument(skip(self), level = \"trace\")]\n   253→    fn regions(&mut self, a: I::Region, b: I::Region) -> RelateResult<I, I::Region> {\n   254→        match self.ambient_variance {\n   255→            // Subtype(&'a u8, &'b u8) => Outlives('a: 'b) => SubRegion('b, 'a)\n   256→            ty::Covariant => self.infcx.sub_regions(b, a, self.span),\n   257→            // Suptype(&'a u8, &'b u8) => Outlives('b: 'a) => SubRegion('a, 'b)\n   258→            ty::Contravariant => self.infcx.sub_regions(a, b, self.span),\n   259→            ty::Invariant => self.infcx.equate_regions(a, b, self.span),\n   260→            ty::Bivariant => {\n   261→                unreachable!(\"Expected bivariance to be handled in relate_with_variance\")\n   262→            }\n   263→        }\n   264→\n   265→        Ok(a)\n   266→    }\n   267→\n   268→    #[instrument(skip(self), level = \"trace\")]\n   269→    fn consts(&mut self, a: I::Const, b: I::Const) -> RelateResult<I, I::Const> {\n   270→        super_combine_consts(self.infcx, self, a, b)\n   271→    }\n   272→\n   273→    fn binders<T>(\n   274→        &mut self,\n   275→        a: ty::Binder<I, T>,\n   276→        b: ty::Binder<I, T>,\n   277→    ) -> RelateResult<I, ty::Binder<I, T>>\n   278→    where\n   279→        T: Relate<I>,\n   280→    {\n   281→        // If they're equal, then short-circuit.\n   282→        if a == b {\n   283→            return Ok(a);\n   284→        }\n   285→\n   286→        // If they have no bound vars, relate normally.\n   287→        if let Some(a_inner) = a.no_bound_vars()\n   288→            && let Some(b_inner) = b.no_bound_vars()\n   289→        {\n   290→            self.relate(a_inner, b_inner)?;\n   291→            return Ok(a);\n   292→        }\n   293→\n   294→        match self.ambient_variance {\n   295→            // Checks whether `for<..> sub <: for<..> sup` holds.\n   296→            //\n   297→            // For this to hold, **all** instantiations of the super type\n   298→            // have to be a super type of **at least one** instantiation of\n   299→            // the subtype.\n   300→            //\n   301→            // This is implemented by first entering a new universe.\n   302→            // We then replace all bound variables in `sup` with placeholders,\n   303→            // and all bound variables in `sub` with inference vars.\n   304→            // We can then just relate the two resulting types as normal.\n   305→            //\n   306→            // Note: this is a subtle algorithm. For a full explanation, please see\n   307→            // the [rustc dev guide][rd]\n   308→            //\n   309→            // [rd]: https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/borrow_check/region_inference/placeholders_and_universes.html\n   310→            ty::Covariant => {\n   311→                self.infcx.enter_forall(b, |b| {\n   312→                    let a = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(a);\n   313→                    self.relate(a, b)\n   314→                })?;\n   315→            }\n   316→            ty::Contravariant => {\n   317→                self.infcx.enter_forall(a, |a| {\n   318→                    let b = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(b);\n   319→                    self.relate(a, b)\n   320→                })?;\n   321→            }\n   322→\n   323→            // When **equating** binders, we check that there is a 1-to-1\n   324→            // correspondence between the bound vars in both types.\n   325→            //\n   326→            // We do so by separately instantiating one of the binders with\n   327→            // placeholders and the other with inference variables and then\n   328→            // equating the instantiated types.\n   329→            //\n   330→            // We want `for<..> A == for<..> B` -- therefore we want\n   331→            // `exists<..> A == for<..> B` and `exists<..> B == for<..> A`.\n   332→            // Check if `exists<..> A == for<..> B`\n   333→            ty::Invariant => {\n   334→                self.infcx.enter_forall(b, |b| {\n   335→                    let a = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(a);\n   336→                    self.relate(a, b)\n   337→                })?;\n   338→\n   339→                // Check if `exists<..> B == for<..> A`.\n   340→                self.infcx.enter_forall(a, |a| {\n   341→                    let b = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(b);\n   342→                    self.relate(a, b)\n   343→                })?;\n   344→            }\n   345→            ty::Bivariant => {\n   346→                unreachable!(\"Expected bivariance to be handled in relate_with_variance\")\n   347→            }\n   348→        }\n   349→        Ok(a)\n   350→    }\n   351→}\n   352→\n   353→impl<Infcx, I> PredicateEmittingRelation<Infcx> for SolverRelating<'_, Infcx, I>\n   354→where\n   355→    Infcx: InferCtxtLike<Interner = I>,\n   356→    I: Interner,\n   357→{\n   358→    fn span(&self) -> I::Span {\n   359→        Span::dummy()\n   360→    }\n   361→\n   362→    fn param_env(&self) -> I::ParamEnv {\n   363→        self.param_env\n   364→    }\n   365→\n   366→    fn structurally_relate_aliases(&self) -> StructurallyRelateAliases {\n   367→        self.structurally_relate_aliases\n   368→    }\n   369→\n   370→    fn register_predicates(\n   371→        &mut self,\n   372→        obligations: impl IntoIterator<Item: ty::Upcast<I, I::Predicate>>,\n   373→    ) {\n   374→        self.goals.extend(\n   375→            obligations.into_iter().map(|pred| Goal::new(self.infcx.cx(), self.param_env, pred)),\n   376→        );\n   377→    }\n   378→\n   379→    fn register_goals(&mut self, obligations: impl IntoIterator<Item = Goal<I, I::Predicate>>) {\n   380→        self.goals.extend(obligations);\n   381→    }\n   382→\n   383→    fn register_alias_relate_predicate(&mut self, a: I::Ty, b: I::Ty) {\n   384→        self.register_predicates([ty::Binder::dummy(match self.ambient_variance {\n   385→            ty::Covariant => ty::PredicateKind::AliasRelate(\n   386→                a.into(),\n   387→                b.into(),\n   388→                ty::AliasRelationDirection::Subtype,\n   389→            ),\n   390→            // a :> b is b <: a\n   391→            ty::Contravariant => ty::PredicateKind::AliasRelate(\n   392→                b.into(),\n   393→                a.into(),\n   394→                ty::AliasRelationDirection::Subtype,\n   395→            ),\n   396→            ty::Invariant => ty::PredicateKind::AliasRelate(\n   397→                a.into(),\n   398→                b.into(),\n   399→                ty::AliasRelationDirection::Equate,\n   400→            ),\n   401→            ty::Bivariant => {\n   402→                unreachable!(\"Expected bivariance to be handled in relate_with_variance\")\n   403→            }\n   404→        })]);\n   405→ 141→\n   142→#[derive(Debug)]\n   143→pub struct FlagComputation<I> {\n   144→    pub flags: TypeFlags,\n   145→\n   146→    /// see `Ty::outer_exclusive_binder` for details\n   147→    pub     let mut result = FlagComputation::new();\n   164→        result.add_kind(kind);\n   165→        result\n   166→    }\n   167→\n   168→    pub fn for_predicate(binder: ty::Binder<I, 174→    pub fn for_const_kind(kind: &ty::ConstKind<I>) -> FlagComputation<I> {\n   175→        let mut result = FlagComputation::new();\n   176→        result.add_const_kind(kind);\n   177→        result\n   178→    }\n   179→\n   180→    pub fn for_clauses(clauses: &[I::Clause]) -> FlagComputation<I> {\n   181→        let mut result = FlagComputation::new();\n   182→        for c in clauses {\n   183→            result.add_flags(c.as_predicate().flags());\n   184→            result.add_exclusive_binder(c.as_predicate().outer_exclusive_binder());\n   185→        }\n   186→        result\n   187→    }\n   188→\n   189→    fn add_flags(&mut self, flags: TypeFlags) {\n   190→        self.flags = self.flags | flags;\n   191→    }\n   192→\n   193→    /// indicates that `self` refers to something at binding level `binder`\n   194→    fn add_bound_var(&mut self, binder: ty::DebruijnIndex) {\n   195→        let exclusive_binder = binder.shifted_in(1);\n   196→        self.add_exclusive_binder(exclusive_binder);\n   197→    }\n   198→\n   199→    /// indicates that `self` refers to something *inside* binding\n   200→    /// level `binder` -- not bound by `binder`, but bound by the next\n   201→    /// binder internal to it\n   202→    fn add_exclusive_binder(&mut self, exclusive_binder: ty::DebruijnIndex) {\n   203→        self.outer_exclusive_binder = self.outer_exclusive_binder.max(exclusive_binder);\n   204→    }\n   205→\n   206→    /// Adds the flags/depth from a set of types that appear within the current type, but within a\n   207→    /// region binder.\n   208→    fn bound_computation<T, F>(&mut self, value: ty::Binder<I, T>, f: F)\n   209→    where\n   210→        F: FnOnce(&mut Self, T),\n   211→    {\n   212→        let mut computation = FlagComputation::new();\n   213→\n   214→        if !value.bound_vars().is_empty() {\n   215→            computation.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_BINDER_VARS);\n   216→        }\n   217→\n   218→        f(&mut computation, value.skip_binder());\n   219→\n   220→        self.add_flags(computation.flags);\n   221→\n   222→        // The types that contributed to `computation` occurred within\n   223→        // a region binder, so subtract one from the region depth\n   224→        // within when adding the depth to `self`.\n   225→        let outer_exclusive_binder = computation.outer_exclusive_binder;\n   226→        if outer_exclusive_binder > ty::INNERMOST {\n   227→            self.add_exclusive_binder(outer_exclusive_binder.shifted_out(1));\n   228→        } // otherwise, this binder captures nothing\n   229→    }\n   230→\n   231→    #[allow(rustc::usage_of_ty_tykind)]\n   232→    fn add_kind(&mut self, kind: &ty::TyKind<I>) {\n   233→        match *kind {\n   234→            ty::Bool\n   235→            | ty::Char\n   236→            | ty::Int(_)\n   237→            | ty::Float(_)\n   238→            | ty::Uint(_)\n   239→            | ty::Never\n   240→            | ty::Str\n   241→            | ty::Foreign(..) => {}\n   242→\n   243→            ty::Error(_) => self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_ERROR),\n   244→\n   245→            ty::Param(_) => {\n   246→                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_PARAM);\n   247→            }\n   248→\n   249→            ty::Closure(_, args)\n   250→            | ty::CoroutineClosure(_, args)\n   251→            | ty::CoroutineWitness(_, args) => {\n   252→                self.add_args(args.as_slice());\n   253→            }\n   254→\n   255→            ty::Coroutine(_, args) => {\n   256→                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_CORO);\n   257→                self.add_args(args.as_slice());\n   258→            }\n   259→\n   260→            ty::Bound(ty::BoundVarIndexKind::Canonical, _) => {\n   261→                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_BOUND);\n   262→                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_CANONICAL_BOUND);\n   263→            }\n   264→\n   265→            ty::Bound(ty::BoundVarIndexKind::Bound(debruijn), _) => {\n   266→                self.add_bound_var(debruijn);\n   267→                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_BOUND);\n   268→            }\n   269→\n   270→            ty::Placeholder(..) => {\n   271→                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_PLACEHOLDER);\n   272→            }\n   273→\n   274→            ty::Infer(infer) => match infer {\n   275→                ty::FreshTy(_) | ty::FreshIntTy(_) | ty::FreshFloatTy(_) => {\n   276→                    self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_FRESH)\n   277→                }\n   278→\n   279→                ty::TyVar(_) | ty::IntVar(_) | ty::FloatVar(_) => {\n   280→                    self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_INFER)\n   281→                }\n   282→            },\n   283→\n   284→            ty::Adt(_, args) => {\n   285→                self.add_args(args.as_slice());\n   286→            }\n   287→\n   288→            ty::Alias(kind, data) => {\n   289→                self.add_flags(match kind {\n   290→                    ty::Projection => TypeFlags::HAS_TY_PROJECTION,\n   291→                    ty::Free => TypeFlags::HAS_TY_FREE_ALIAS,\n   292→                    ty::Opaque => TypeFlags::HAS_TY_OPAQUE,\n   293→                    ty::Inherent => TypeFlags::HAS_TY_INHERENT,\n   294→                });\n   295→\n   296→                self.add_alias_ty(data);\n   297→            }\n   298→\n   299→            ty::Dynamic(obj, r) => {\n   300→                for predicate in obj.iter() {\n   301→                    self.bound_computation(predicate, |computation, predicate| match predicate {\n   302→                        ty::ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => {\n   303→                            computation.add_args(tr.args.as_slice())\n   304→                        }\n   305→                        ty::ExistentialPredicate::Projection(p) => {\n   306→                            computation.add_existential_projection(&p);\n   307→                        }\n   308→                        ty::ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_) => {}\n   309→                    });\n   310→                }\n   311→\n   312→                self.add_region(r);\n   313→            }\n   314→\n   315→            ty::Array(tt, len) => {\n   316→                self.add_ty(tt);\n   317→                self.add_const(len);\n   318→            }\n   319→\n   320→            ty::Pat(ty, pat) => {\n   321→                self.add_ty(ty);\n   322→                self.add_ty_pat(pat);\n   323→            }\n   324→\n   325→            ty::Slice(tt) => self.add_ty(tt),\n   326→\n   327→            ty::RawPtr(ty, _) => {\n   328→                self.add_ty(ty);\n   329→            }\n   330→\n   331→            ty::Ref(r, ty, _) => {\n   332→                self.add_region(r);\n   333→                self.add_ty(ty);\n   334→            }\n   335→\n   336→            ty::Tuple(types) => {\n   337→   ty::FnPtr(sig_tys, _) => self.bound_computation(sig_tys, |computation, sig_tys| {\n   345→                computation.add_tys(sig_tys.inputs_and_output);\n   346→            }),\n   347→\n   348→            ty::UnsafeBinder(bound_ty) => {\n   349→                self.bound_computation(bound_ty.into(), |computation, ty| {\n   350→                    computation.add_ty(ty);\n   351→                })\n   352→            }\n   353→        }\n   354→    }\n   355→\n   356→    fn add_ty_pat(&mut self, pat: <I as Interner>::Pat) {\n   357→        self.add_flags(pat.flags());\n   358→        self.add_exclusive_binder(pat.outer_exclusive_binder());\n   359→    }\n   360→\n   361→    fn add_predicate(&mut self, binder: ty::Binder<I, ty::PredicateKind<I>>) {\n   362→        self.bound_computation(binder, |computation, atom| computation.add_predicate_atom(atom));\n   363→    }\n   364→\n   365→    fn add_predicate_atom(&mut self, atom: ty::PredicateKind<I>) {\n   366→ }\n   416→            ty::PredicateKind::ConstEquate(expected, found) => {\n   417→                self.add_const(expected);\n   418→                self.add_const(found);\n   419→            }\n }\n   450→\n   451→    fn add_const(&mut self, c: I::Const) {\n   452→        self.add_flags(c.flags());\n   453→        self.add_exclusive_binder(c.outer_exclusive_binder());\n   454→    }\n   455→\n   456→    fn add_const_kind(&mut self, c: &ty::ConstKind<I>) {\n   457→        match *c {\n   458→            ty::ConstKind::Unevaluated(uv) => {\n   459→                ty::InferConst::Fresh(_) => self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_CT_FRESH),\n   464→                ty::InferConst::Var(_) => self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_CT_INFER),\n   465→            },\n        self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_CT_PARAM);\n   476→            }\n   477→            ty::ConstKind::Placeholder(_) => {\n   478→                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_CT_PLACEHOLDER);\n ty::ConstKind::Error(_) => self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_ERROR),\n   483→        }\n   484→    }\n   485→\n   486→    fn add_existential_projection(&mut self, projection: &ty::ExistentialProjection<I>) {\n   487→        self.add_args(projection.args.as_slice());\n   488→        match projection.term.kind() {\n   489→            ty::TermKind::Ty(ty) => (QueryResult<Self>, Self::Probe);\n   415→}\n   416→\n   417→/// Imagine you have a function `F: FnOnce(&[T]) -> R`, plus an iterator `iter`\n   418→/// that produces `T` items. You could combine them with\n   419→/// `f(&iter.collect::<Vec<_>>())`, but this requires allocating memory for the\n   420→/// `Vec`.\n   421→///\n   422→/// This trait allows for faster implementations, intended for cases where the\n   423→/// number of items produced by the iterator is small. There is a blanket impl\n   424→/// for `T` items, but there is also a fallible impl for `Result<T, E>` items.\n   425→pub trait CollectAndApply<T, R>: Sized {\n   426→    type Output;\n   427→\n   428→    /// Produce a result of type `Self::Output` from `iter`. The result will\n   429→    /// typically be produced by applying `f` on the elements produced by\n   430→    /// `iter`, though this may not happen in some impls, e.g. if an error\n   431→    /// occurred during iteration.\n   432→    fn collect_and_apply<I, F>(iter: I, f: F) -> Self::Output\n   433→    where\n   434→        I: Iterator<Item = Self>,\n   435→        F: FnOnce(&[T]) -> R;\n   436→}\n   437→\n   438→/// The blanket impl that always collects all elements and applies `f`.\n   439→impl<T, R> CollectAndApply<T, R> for T {\n   440→    type Output = R;\n   441→\n   442→    /// Equivalent to `f(&iter.collect::<Vec<_>>())`.\n   443→    fn collect_and_apply<I, F>(mut iter: I, f: F) -> R\n   444→    where\n   445→        I: Iterator<Item = T>,\n   446→        F: FnOnce(&[T]) -> R,\n   447→    {\n   448→        // This code is hot enough that it's worth specializing for the most\n   449→        // common length lists, to avoid the overhead of `Vec` creation.\n   450→\n   451→       return f(&[t0, t1, t2]);\n   465→        };\n   466→\n   467→        let Some(t4) = iter.next() else {\n   468→            return f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3]);\n   469→        };\n   470→\n   471→ t1, t2, t3, t4, t5]);\n   477→        };\n   478→\n   479→        let Some(t7) = iter.next() else {\n   480→            return f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4, t5, t6]);\n   481→        };\n   482→\n   483→        let Some(t8) = iter.next() else {\n   484→            return f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4, t5, t6, t7]);\n   485→        };\n   486→\n   487→        f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4, t5, t6, t7, t8].into_iter().chain(iter).collect::<Vec<_>>())\n   488→    }\n   489→}\n   490→\n   491→/// A fallible impl that will fail, without calling `f`, if there are any\n   492→/// errors during collection.\n   493→impl<T, R, E> CollectAndApply<T, R> for Result<T, E> {\n   494→    type Output = Result<R, E>;\n   495→\n   496→    /// Equivalent to `Ok(f(&iter.collect::<Result<Vec<_>>>()?))`.\n   497→    fn collect_and_apply<I, F>(mut iter: I, f: F) -> Result<R, E>\n   498→    where\n   499→        I: Iterator<Item = Result<T, E>>,\n   500→        F: FnOnce(&[T]) -> R,\n   501→    {\n   502→ Ok(f(&[t0]));\n   512→        };\n   513→        let t1 = t1?;\n   514→\n   515→        let Some(t2) = iter.next() else {\n   516→            return Ok(f(&[t0, t1]));\n   517→        };\n   518→ Whether a goal is added\n    34→    /// once or multiple times is not really meaningful.\n    35→    ///\n    36→    /// Changes in the inference state may delay some type inference to\n    37→    /// the next fulfillment loop. Given that this loop is already\n    38→    /// necessary, this is also not a meaningful change. Consider\n    39→    /// the following three relations:\n    40→    /// ```text\n    41→    /// Vec<?0> sub Vec<?1>\n    42→    /// ?0 eq u32\n    43→    /// Vec<?0> sub Vec<?1>\n    44→    /// ```\n    45→    /// Without a cache, the second `Vec<?0> sub Vec<?1>` would eagerly\n    46→    /// constrain `?1` to `u32`. When using the cache entry from the\n    47→    /// first time we've related these types, this only happens when\n    48→    /// later proving the `Subtype(?0, ?1)` goal from the first relation.\n    49→    cache: DelayedSet<(ty::Variance, Ty<'tcx>, Ty<'tcx>)>,\n    50→}\n    51→\n    52→impl<'infcx, 'tcx> TypeRelating<'infcx, 'tcx> {\n    53→    pub(crate) fn new(\n    54→        infcx: &'infcx We do this per var because we only really need one\n   386→        // suggestion to influence a var to be `()`.\n   387→        let suggestions = diverging_vids\n   388→            .iter()\n   389→            .copied()\n   390→            .filter_map(|vid| {\n   391→                let reachable_vids =\n   392→                    graph::depth_first_search_as_undirected(coercions, vid).collect();\n   393→                AnnotateUnitFallbackVisitor { reachable_vids, fcx: self }\n   394→                    .visit_expr(body.value)\n   395→                    .break_value()\n   396→            })\n   397→            .collect();\n   398→        errors::SuggestAnnotations { suggestions }\n   399→    }\n   400→}\n   401→\n   402→/// Try to walk the HIR to find a place to insert a useful suggestion\n   403→/// to preserve fallback to `()` in 2024.\n   404→struct AnnotateUnitFallbackVisitor<'a, 'tcx> {\n   405→    reachable_vids: FxHashSet<ty::TyVid>,\n   406→    fcx: &'a FnCtxt<'a, 'tcx>,\n   407→}\n   408→impl<'tcx> AnnotateUnitFallbackVisitor<'_, 'tcx> {\n   409→    // For a given path segment, if it's missing a turbofish, try to suggest adding\n   410→    // one so we can constrain an argument to `()`. To keep the suggestion simple,\n   411→    // we want to simply suggest `_` for all the other args. This (for now) only\n   412→    // works when there are only type variables (and region variables, since we can\n   413→    // elide them)...\n   414→    fn suggest_for_segment(\n   415→        &self,\n   416→        arg_segment: &'tcx hir::PathSegment<'tcx>,\n   417→        def_id: DefId,\n   418→        id: HirId,\n   419→    ) -> ControlFlow<errors::SuggestAnnotation> {\n   420→        if arg_segment.args.is_none()\n   421→            && let Some(all_args) = self.fcx.typeck_results.borrow().node_args_opt(id)\n   422→            && let generics = self.fcx.tcx.generics_of(def_id)\n   423→            && let args = all_args[generics.parent_count..].iter().zip(&generics.own_params)\n   424→            // We can't turbofish consts :(\n   425→            && args.clone().all(|(_, param)| matches!(param.kind, ty::GenericParamDefKind::Type { .. } | ty::GenericParamDefKind::Lifetime))\n   426→        {\n   427→            // We filter out APITs, which are not turbofished.\n   428→            let non_apit_type_args = args.filter(|(_, param)| {\n   429→                matches!(param.kind, ty::GenericParamDefKind::Type { synthetic: false, .. })\n   430→            });\n   431→            let n_tys = non_apit_type_args.clone().count();\n   432→            for (idx, (arg, _)) in non_apit_type_args.enumerate() {\n   433→                if let Some(ty) = arg.as_type()\n   434→                    && let Some(vid) = self.fcx.root_vid(ty)\n   435→                    && self.reachable_vids.contains(&vid)\n   436→                {\n   437→                    return ControlFlow::Break(errors::SuggestAnnotation::Turbo(\n   438→                        arg_segment.ident.span.shrink_to_hi(),\n   439→                        n_tys,\n   440→                        idx,\n   441→                    ));\n   442→                }\n   443→            }\n   444→        }\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. These take numerous guises in syntax,\n/// but perhaps the most recognizable form is in a where-clause:\n/// ```ignore (illustrative)\n/// T: Foo<U>\n/// ```\n/// This would be represented by a trait-reference where the `DefId` is the\n/// `DefId` for the trait `Foo` and the args define `T` as parameter 0,\n/// and `U` as parameter 1.\n///\n/// Trait references also appear in object types TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub struct TraitRef<I: Interner> {\n    pub def_id: I::TraitId,\n    pub args: I::GenericArgs,\n    /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `TraitRef` without\n    /// calling [`TraitRef::new_from_args`].\n    _use_trait_ref_new_instead: (),\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for TraitRef<I> {}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> TraitRef<I> {\n    pub fn new_from_args(interner: I, trait_def_id: I::TraitId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> Self {\n        interner.debug_assert_args_compatible(trait_def_id.into(), args);\n        Self { def_id: trait_def_id, args, _use_trait_ref_new_instead: () }\n    }\n\n    pub fn new(\n        interner: I,\n        trait_def_id: I::TraitId,\n        args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\n    ) -> Self {\n        let args = I::GenericArgs) -> TraitRef<I> {\n        let generics = interner.generics_of(trait_id.into());\n        TraitRef::new(interner, trait_id, args.iter().take(generics.count()))\n    }\n\n    /// Returns a `TraitRef` of the form `P0: Foo<P1..Pn>` where `Pi`\n    /// are the parameters defined on trait.\n    pub fn identity(interner: I, def_id: I::TraitId) -> TraitRef<I> {\n self.skip_binder().def_id\n    }\n\n    pub fn to_host_effect_clause(self, cx: I, constness: BoundConstness) -> I::Clause {\n        self.map_bound(|trait_ref| {\n HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub struct TraitPredicate<I: Interner> {\n    pub trait_ref: TraitRef<I>,\n\n    /// If polarity is Positive: we are proving that the trait is implemented.\n    ///\n    /// If polarity is Negative: we are proving that a negative impl of this trait\n    /// exists. (Note that coherence also checks whether negative impls of supertraits\n    /// exist via a series of predicates.)\n    pub polarity: PredicatePolarity,\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for TraitPredicate<I> {}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> TraitPredicate<I> {\n    pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> Self {\n        Self {\n            trait_ref: pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\n        self.trait_ref.self_ty()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, TraitPredicate<I>> {\n    pub fn def_id(self) -> I::TraitId {\n        // Ok to skip Debug)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub enum ImplPolarity {\n    /// `impl Trait for Type`\n    Positive,\n    /// `impl !Trait for Type`\n    Negative,\n    /// `#[rustc_reservation_impl] impl Trait for Type`\n    ///\n    /// This is a \"stability hack\", not a real Rust feature.\n    /// See #64631 for details.\n    Reservation,\n}\n\nimpl fmt::Display for ImplPolarity {\n    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\n        match self {\n            Self::Positive => f.write_str(\"positive\"),\n            Self::Negative => f.write_str(\"negative\"),\n            Self::Reservation => f.write_str(\"reservation\"),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl ImplPolarity {\n    /// The polarity marker in front of the impl trait ref if applicable.\n    pub fn as_str(self) -> &'static str {\n        match self {\n            Self::Positive => \"\",\n            Self::Negative => \"!\",\n            Self::Reservation => \"\",\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n/// Polarity for a trait predicate. May either be negative or positive.\n/// Distinguished from [`ImplPolarity`] since we never compute goals with\n/// \"reservation\" level.\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Debug)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub enum PredicatePolarity {\n    /// `Type: Trait`\n    Positive,\n    /// `Type: !Trait`\n    Negative,\n}\n\nimpl PredicatePolarity {\n    /// Flips polarity by turning `Positive` into `Negative` and `Negative` into `Positive`.\n    pub fn flip(&self) -> PredicatePolarity {\n        match self {\n            PredicatePolarity::Positive => PredicatePolarity::Negative,\n            PredicatePolarity::Negative => PredicatePolarity::Positive,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl fmt::Display for PredicatePolarity {\n    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\n        match self {\n            Self::Positive => f.write_str(\"positive\"),\n            Self::Negative => f.write_str(\"negative\"),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\n#[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub enum ExistentialPredicate<I: Interner> {\n    /// E.g., `Iterator`.\n    Trait(ExistentialTraitRef<I>),\n    /// E.g., `Iterator::Item = T`.\n    Projection(ExistentialProjection<I>),\n    /// E.g., `Send`.\n    AutoTrait(I::TraitId),\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for ExistentialPredicate<I> {}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialPredicate<I>> {\n    /// Given an existential predicate like `?Self: PartialEq<u32>` (e.g., derived from `dyn PartialEq<u32>`),\n    /// and a concrete type `self_ty`, returns a full predicate where the existentially quantified variable `?Self`\n    /// has been replaced with `self_ty` (e.g., `self_ty: PartialEq<u32>`, in our example).\n    pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> I::Clause {\n        match self.skip_binder() {\n            ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => self.rebind(tr).with_self_ty(cx, self_ty).upcast(cx),\n            ExistentialPredicate::Projection(p) => {\n                self.rebind(p.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty)).upcast(cx)\n            }\n            ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(did) => {\n                let generics = cx.generics_of(did.into());\n                let trait_ref = if generics.count() == 1 {\n                    ty::TraitRef::new(cx, did, [self_ty])\n                } else {\n                    // If this is an ill-formed auto trait, then synthesize\n                    // new error args for the missing generics.\n                    let err_args =\n                        GenericArgs::extend_with_error(cx, did.into(), &[self_ty.into()]);\n                    ty::TraitRef::new_from_args(cx, did, err_args)\n                };\n                self.rebind(trait_ref).upcast(cx)\n            }\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n/// An existential reference to a trait, where `Self` is erased.\n/// For example, the trait object `Trait<'a, 'b, X, Y>` is:\n/// ```ignore (illustrative)\n/// exists T. T: Trait<'a, 'b, X, Y>\n/// ```\n/// The generic parameters don't include the erased `Self`, only trait\n/// type and lifetime parameters (`[X, Y]` and `['a, 'b]` above).\n#[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\n#[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub struct ExistentialTraitRef<I: Interner> {\n    pub def_id: I::TraitId,\n    pub args: I::GenericArgs,\n    /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `ExistentialTraitRef` without\n    /// calling [`ExistentialTraitRef::new_from_args`].\n    _use_existential_trait_ref_new_instead: (),\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for ExistentialTraitRef<I> {}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\n    pub fn new_from_args(interner: I, trait_def_id: I::TraitId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> Self {\n        interner.debug_assert_existential_args_compatible(trait_def_id.into(), args);\n        Self { def_id: trait_def_id, args, _use_existential_trait_ref_new_instead: () }\n    }\n\n    pub fn new(\n        interner: I,\n        trait_def_id: I::TraitId,\n        args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\n    ) -> Self {\n        let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\n        Self::new_from_args(interner, trait_def_id, args)\n    }\n\n    pub fn erase_self_ty(interner: I, trait_ref: TraitRef<I>) -> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\n        // Assert there is a Self.\n        trait_ref.args.type_at(0);\n\n        ExistentialTraitRef {\n            def_id: trait_ref.def_id,\n            args: interner.mk_args(&trait_ref.args.as_slice()[1..]),\n            _use_existential_trait_ref_new_instead: (),\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Object types don't have a self type specified. Therefore, when\n    /// we convert the principal trait-ref into a normal trait-ref,\n    /// you must give *some* self type. A common choice is `mk_err()`\n    /// or some placeholder type.\n    pub fn with_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> TraitRef<I> {\n        // otherwise the escaping vars would be captured by the binder\n        // debug_assert!(!self_ty.has_escaping_bound_vars());\n\n        TraitRef::new(interner, self.def_id, [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter()))\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialTraitRef<I>> {\n    pub fn def_id(&self) -> I::TraitId {\n        self.skip_binder().def_id\n    }\n\n    /// Object types don't have a self type specified. Therefore, when\n    /// we convert the principal trait-ref into a normal trait-ref,\n    /// you must give *some* self type. A common choice is `mk_err()`\n    /// or some placeholder type.\n    pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ty::Binder<I, TraitRef<I>> {\n        self.map_bound(|trait_ref| trait_ref.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty))\n    }\n}\n\n/// A `ProjectionPredicate` for an `ExistentialTraitRef`.\n#[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\n#[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub struct ExistentialProjection<I: Interner> {\n    pub def_id: I::DefId,\n    pub args: I::GenericArgs,\n    pub term: I::Term,\n\n    /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `ExistentialProjection`\n    /// without using [`ExistentialProjection::new_from_args`].\n    use_existential_projection_new_instead: (),\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for ExistentialProjection<I> {}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> ExistentialProjection<I> {\n    pub fn new_from_args(\n        interner: I,\n        def_id: I::DefId,\n        args: I::GenericArgs,\n        term: I::Term,\n    ) -> ExistentialProjection<I> {\n        interner.debug_assert_existential_args_compatible(def_id, args);\n        Self { def_id, args, term, use_existential_projection_new_instead: () }\n    }\n\n    pub fn new(\n        interner: I,\n        def_id: I::DefId,\n        args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\n        term: I::Term,\n    ) -> ExistentialProjection<I> {\n        let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\n        Self::new_from_args(interner, def_id, args, term)\n    }\n\n    /// Extracts the underlying existential trait reference from this projection.\n    /// For example, if this is a projection of `exists T. <T as Iterator>::Item == X`,\n    /// then this function would return an `exists T. T: Iterator` existential trait\n    /// reference.\n    pub fn trait_ref(&self, interner: I) -> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\n        let def_id = interner.parent(self.def_id);\n        let args_count = interner.generics_of(def_id).count() - 1;\n        let args = interner.mk_args(&self.args.as_slice()[..args_count]);\n        ExistentialTraitRef::new_from_args(interner, def_id.try_into().unwrap(), args)\n    }\n\n    pub fn with_self_ty(&self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\n        // otherwise the escaping regions would be captured by the binders\n        debug_assert!(!self_ty.has_escaping_bound_vars());\n\n        ProjectionPredicate {\n            projection_term: AliasTerm::new(\n                interner,\n                self.def_id,\n                [self_ty.into()].iter().chain(self.args.iter()),\n            ),\n            term: self.term,\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn erase_self_ty(interner: I, projection_predicate: ProjectionPredicate<I>) -> Self {\n        // Assert there is a Self.\n        projection_predicate.projection_term.args.type_at(0);\n\n        Self {\n            def_id: projection_predicate.projection_term.def_id,\n            args: interner.mk_args(&projection_predicate.projection_term.args.as_slice()[1..]),\n            term: projection_predicate.term,\n            use_existential_projection_new_instead: (),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialProjection<I>> {\n    pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ty::Binder<I, ProjectionPredicate<I>> {\n        self.map_bound(|p| p.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty))\n    }\n\n    pub fn item_def_id(&self) -> I::DefId {\n        self.skip_binder().def_id\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Debug)]\n#[cfg_attr(feature = \"nightly\", derive(Encodable, Decodable, HashStable_NoContext))]\npub enum AliasTermKind {\n    /// A projection `<Type as Trait>::AssocType`.\n    /// Can get normalized away if monomorphic enough.\n    ProjectionTy,\n    /// An associated type in an inherent `impl`\n    InherentTy,\n    /// An opaque type (usually from `impl Trait` in type aliases or function return types)\n    /// Can only be normalized away in PostAnalysis mode or its defining scope.\n    OpaqueTy,\n    /// A free type alias that actually checks its trait bounds.\n    /// Currently only used if the type alias references opaque types.\n    /// Can always be normalized away.\n    FreeTy,\n\n    /// An unevaluated anonymous constants.\n    UnevaluatedConst,\n    /// An unevaluated const coming from an associated const.\n    ProjectionConst,\n    /// A top level const item not part of a trait or impl.\n    FreeConst,\n    /// An associated const in an inherent `impl`\n    InherentConst,\n}\n\nimpl AliasTermKind {\n    pub fn descr(self) -> &'static str {\n        match self {\n            AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => \"associated type\",\n            AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => \"associated const\",\n            AliasTermKind::InherentTy => \"inherent associated type\",\n            AliasTermKind::InherentConst => \"inherent associated const\",\n            AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => \"opaque type\",\n            AliasTermKind::FreeTy => \"type alias\",\n            AliasTermKind::FreeConst => \"unevaluated constant\",\n            AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst => \"unevaluated constant\",\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn is_type(self) -> bool {\n        match self {\n            AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\n            | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\n            | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\n            | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => true,\n\n            AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\n            | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\n            | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n            | AliasTermKind::FreeConst => false,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl From<ty::AliasTyKind> for AliasTermKind {\n    fn from(value: ty::AliasTyKind) -> Self {\n        match value {\n            ty::Projection => AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy,\n            ty::Opaque => AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy,\n            ty::Free => AliasTermKind::FreeTy,\n            ty::Inherent => AliasTermKind::InherentTy,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n/// Represents the unprojected term of a projection goal.\n///\n/// * For a projection, this would be `<Ty as Trait<...>>::N<...>`.\n/// * For an inherent projection, this would be `Ty::N<...>`.\n/// * For an opaque type, there is no explicit syntax.\n#[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\n#[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub struct AliasTerm<I: Interner> {\n    /// The parameters of the associated or opaque item.\n    ///\n    /// For a projection, these are the generic parameters for the trait and the\n    /// GAT parameters, if there are any.\n    ///\n    /// For an inherent projection, they consist of the self type and the GAT parameters,\n    /// if there are any.\n    ///\n    /// For RPIT the generic parameters are for the generics of the function,\n    /// while for TAIT it is used for the generic parameters of the alias.\n    pub args: I::GenericArgs,\n\n    /// The `DefId` of the `TraitItem` or `ImplItem` for the associated type `N` depending on whether\n    /// this is a projection or an inherent projection or the `DefId` of the `OpaqueType` item if\n    /// this is an opaque.\n    ///\n    /// During codegen, `interner.type_of(def_id)` can be used to get the type of the\n    /// underlying type if the type is an opaque.\n    ///\n    /// Note that if this is an associated type, this is not the `DefId` of the\n    /// `TraitRef` containing this associated type, which is in `interner.associated_item(def_id).container`,\n    /// aka. `interner.parent(def_id)`.\n    pub def_id: I::DefId,\n\n    /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `AliasTerm` without using [`AliasTerm::new_from_args`].\n    #[derive_where(skip(Debug))]\n    _use_alias_term_new_instead: (),\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for AliasTerm<I> {}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\n    pub fn new_from_args(interner: I, def_id: I::DefId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> AliasTerm<I> {\n        interner.debug_assert_args_compatible(def_id, args);\n        AliasTerm { def_id, args, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\n    }\n\n    pub fn new(\n        interner: I,\n        def_id: I::DefId,\n        args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\n    ) -> AliasTerm<I> {\n        let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\n        Self::new_from_args(interner, def_id, args)\n    }\n\n    pub fn expect_ty(self, interner: I) -> ty::AliasTy<I> {\n        match self.kind(interner) {\n            AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\n            | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\n            | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\n            | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => {}\n            AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n            | AliasTermKind::FreeConst\n            | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\n            | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => {\n                panic!(\"Cannot turn `UnevaluatedConst` into `AliasTy`\")\n            }\n        }\n        ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () }\n    }\n\n    pub fn kind(self, interner: I) -> AliasTermKind {\n        interner.alias_term_kind(self)\n    }\n\n    pub fn to_term(self, interner: I) -> I::Term {\n        match self.kind(interner) {\n            AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => Ty::new_alias(\n                interner,\n                ty::AliasTyKind::Projection,\n                ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n            )\n            .into(),\n            AliasTermKind::InherentTy => Ty::new_alias(\n                interner,\n                ty::AliasTyKind::Inherent,\n                ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n            )\n            .into(),\n            AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => Ty::new_alias(\n                interner,\n                ty::AliasTyKind::Opaque,\n                ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n            )\n            .into(),\n            AliasTermKind::FreeTy => Ty::new_alias(\n                interner,\n                ty::AliasTyKind::Free,\n                ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n            )\n            .into(),\n            AliasTermKind::FreeConst\n            | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n            | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\n            | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => I::Const::new_unevaluated(\n                interner,\n                ty::UnevaluatedConst::new(self.def_id.try_into().unwrap(), self.args),\n            )\n            .into(),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\n/// The following methods work only with (trait) associated term projections.\nimpl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\n    pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\n        self.args.type_at(0)\n    }\n\n    pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> Self {\n        AliasTerm::new(\n            interner,\n            self.def_id,\n            [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)),\n        )\n    }\n\n    pub fn trait_def_id(self, interner: I) -> I::TraitId {\n        assert!(\n            matches!(\n                self.kind(interner),\n                AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\n            ),\n            \"expected a projection\"\n        );\n        interner.parent(self.def_id).try_into().unwrap()\n    }\n\n    /// Extracts the underlying trait reference and own args from this projection.\n    /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as StreamingIterator>::Item<'a>`,\n    /// then this function would return a `T: StreamingIterator` trait reference and\n    /// `['a]` as the own args.\n    pub fn trait_ref_and_own_args(self, interner: I) -> (TraitRef<I>, I::GenericArgsSlice) {\n        interner.trait_ref_and_own_args_for_alias(self.def_id, self.args)\n    }\n\n    /// Extracts the underlying trait reference from this projection.\n    /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as Iterator>::Item`,\n    /// then this function would return a `T: Iterator` trait reference.\n    ///\n    /// WARNING: This will drop the args for generic associated types\n    /// consider calling [Self::trait_ref_and_own_args] to get those\n    /// as well.\n    pub fn trait_ref(self, interner: I) -> TraitRef<I> {\n        self.trait_ref_and_own_args(interner).0\n    }\n\n    /// Extract the own args from this projection.\n    /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as StreamingIterator>::Item<'a>`,\n    /// then this function would return the slice `['a]` as the own args.\n    pub fn own_args(self, interner: I) -> I::GenericArgsSlice {\n        self.trait_ref_and_own_args(interner).1\n    }\n}\n\n/// The following methods work only with inherent associated term projections.\nimpl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\n    /// Transform the generic parameters to have the given `impl` args as the base and the GAT args on top of that.\n    ///\n    /// Does the following transformation:\n    ///\n    /// ```text\n    /// [Self, P_0...P_m] -> [I_0...I_n, P_0...P_m]\n    ///\n    ///     I_i impl args\n    ///     P_j GAT args\n    /// ```\n    pub fn rebase_inherent_args_onto_impl(\n        self,\n        impl_args: I::GenericArgs,\n        interner: I,\n    ) -> I::GenericArgs {\n        debug_assert!(matches!(\n            self.kind(interner),\n            AliasTermKind::InherentTy | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n        ));\n        interner.mk_args_from_iter(impl_args.iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)))\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> From<ty::AliasTy<I>> for AliasTerm<I> {\n    fn from(ty: ty::AliasTy<I>) -> Self {\n        AliasTerm { args: ty.args, def_id: ty.def_id, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> From<ty::UnevaluatedConst<I>> for AliasTerm<I> {\n    fn from(ct: ty::UnevaluatedConst<I>) -> Self {\n        AliasTerm { args: ct.args, def_id: ct.def.into(), _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\n    }\n}\n\n/// This kind of predicate has no *direct* correspondent in the\n/// syntax, but it roughly corresponds to the syntactic forms:\n///\n/// 1. `T: TraitRef<..., Item = Type>`\n/// 2. `<T as TraitRef<...>>::Item == Type` (NYI)\n///\n/// In particular, form #1 is \"desugared\" to the combination of a\n/// normal trait predicate (`T: TraitRef<...>`) and one of these\n/// predicates. Form #2 is a broader form in that it also permits\n/// equality between arbitrary types. Processing an instance of\n/// Form #2 eventually yields one of these `ProjectionPredicate`\n/// instances to normalize the LHS.\n#[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\n#[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub struct ProjectionPredicate<I: Interner> {\n    pub projection_term: AliasTerm<I>,\n    pub term: I::Term,\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for ProjectionPredicate<I> {}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\n    pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\n        self.projection_term.self_ty()\n    }\n\n    pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\n        Self {\n            projection_term: self.projection_term.with_replaced_self_ty(interner, self_ty),\n            ..self\n        }\n    }\n\n    pub fn trait_def_id(self, interner: I) -> I::TraitId {\n        self.projection_term.trait_def_id(interner)\n    }\n\n    pub fn def_id(self) -> I::DefId {\n        self.projection_term.def_id\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ProjectionPredicate<I>> {\n    /// Returns the `DefId` of the trait of the associated item being projected.\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn trait_def_id(&self, cx: I) -> I::TraitId {\n        self.skip_binder().projection_term.trait_def_id(cx)\n    }\n\n    pub fn term(&self) -> ty::Binder<I, I::Term> {\n        self.map_bound(|predicate| predicate.term)\n    }\n\n    /// The `DefId` of the `TraitItem` for the associated type.\n    ///\n    /// Note that this is not the `DefId` of the `TraitRef` containing this\n    /// associated type, which is in `tcx.associated_item(projection_def_id()).container`.\n    pub fn item_def_id(&self) -> I::DefId {\n        // Ok to skip binder since trait `DefId` does not care about regions.\n        self.skip_binder().projection_term.def_id\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> fmt::Debug for ProjectionPredicate<I> {\n    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\n        write!(f, \"ProjectionPredicate({:?}, {:?})\", self.projection_term, self.term)\n    }\n}\n\n/// Used by the new solver to normalize an alias. This always expects the `term` to\n/// be an unconstrained inference variable which is used as the output.\n#[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\n#[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub struct NormalizesTo<I: Interner> {\n    pub alias: AliasTerm<I>,\n    pub term: I::Term,\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for NormalizesTo<I> {}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> NormalizesTo<I> {\n    pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\n        self.alias.self_ty()\n    }\n\n    pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> NormalizesTo<I> {\n        Self { alias: self.alias.with_replaced_self_ty(interner, self_ty), ..self }\n    }\n\n    pub fn trait_def_id(self, interner: I) -> I::TraitId {\n        self.alias.trait_def_id(interner)\n    }\n\n    pub fn def_id(self) -> I::DefId {\n        self.alias.def_id\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> fmt::Debug for NormalizesTo<I> {\n    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\n        write!(f, \"NormalizesTo({:?}, {:?})\", self.alias, self.term)\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\n#[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Encodable_NoContext, Decodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub struct HostEffectPredicate<I: Interner> {\n    pub trait_ref: ty::TraitRef<I>,\n    pub constness: BoundConstness,\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for HostEffectPredicate<I> {}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> HostEffectPredicate<I> {\n    pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\n        self.trait_ref.self_ty()\n    }\n\n    pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> Self {\n        Self { trait_ref: self.trait_ref.with_replaced_self_ty(interner, self_ty), ..self }\n    }\n\n    pub fn def_id(self) -> I::TraitId {\n        self.trait_ref.def_id\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, HostEffectPredicate<I>> {\n    pub fn def_id(self) -> I::TraitId {\n        // Ok to skip binder since trait `DefId` does not care about regions.\n        self.skip_binder().def_id()\n    }\n\n    pub fn self_ty(self) -> ty::Binder<I, I::Ty> {\n        self.map_bound(|trait_ref| trait_ref.self_ty())\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn constness(self) -> BoundConstness {\n        self.skip_binder().constness\n    }\n}\n\n/// Encodes that `a` must be a subtype of `b`. The `a_is_expected` flag indicates\n/// whether the `a` type is the type that we should label as \"expected\" when\n/// presenting user diagnostics.\n#[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\n#[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Encodable_NoContext, Decodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub enum ClauseKind<I: Interner> {\n    /// Corresponds to `where Foo: Bar<A, B, C>`. `Foo` here would be\n    /// the `Self` type of the trait reference and `A`, `B`, and `C`\n    /// would be the type parameters.\n    Trait(ty::TraitPredicate<I>),\n\n    /// `where 'a: 'r`\n    RegionOutlives(ty::OutlivesPredicate<I, I::Region>),\n\n    /// `where T: 'r`\n    TypeOutlives(ty::OutlivesPredicate<I, I::Ty>),\n\n    /// `where <T as TraitRef>::Name == X`, approximately.\n    /// See the `ProjectionPredicate` struct for details.\n    Projection(ty::ProjectionPredicate<I>),\n\n    /// Ensures that a const generic argument to a parameter `const N: u8`\n    /// is of type `u8`.\n    ConstArgHasType(I::Const, I::Ty),\n\n    /// No syntax: `T` well-formed.\n    WellFormed(I::Term),\n\n    /// Constant initializer must evaluate successfully.\n    ConstEvaluatable(I::Const),\n\n    /// Enforces the constness of the predicate we're calling. Like a projection\n    /// goal from a where clause, it's always going to be paired with a\n    /// corresponding trait clause; this just enforces the *constness* of that\n    /// implementation.\n    HostEffect(ty::HostEffectPredicate<I>),\n\n    /// Support marking impl as unstable.\n    UnstableFeature(\n        #[type_foldable(identity)]\n        #[type_visitable(ignore)]\n        I::Symbol,\n    ),\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for ClauseKind<I> {}\n\n#[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\n#[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n    feature = \"nightly\",\n    derive(Encodable_NoContext, Decodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n)]\npub enum PredicateKind<I: Interner> {\n    /// Prove a clause\n    Clause(ClauseKind<I>),\n\n    /// Trait must be dyn-compatible.\n    DynCompatible(I::TraitId),\n\n    /// `T1 <: T2`\n    ///\n    /// This obligation is created most often when we have two\n    /// unresolved type variables and hence don't have enough\n    /// information to process the subtyping obligation yet.\n    Subtype(ty::SubtypePredicate<I>),\n\n    /// `T1` coerced to `T2`\n    ///\n    /// Like a subtyping obligation, this is created most often\n    /// when we have two unresolved type variables and hence\n    /// don't have enough information to process the coercion\n    /// obligation yet. At the moment, we actually process coercions\n    /// very much like subtyping and don't handle the full coercion\n    /// logic.\n    Coerce(ty::CoercePredicate<I>),\n\n    /// Constants must be equal. The first component is the const that is expected.\n    ConstEquate(I::Const, I::Const),\n\n    /// A marker predicate that is always ambiguous.\n    /// Used for coherence to mark opaque types as possibly equal to each other but ambiguous.\n    Ambiguous,\n\n    /// This should only be used inside of the new solver for `AliasRelate` and expects\n    /// the `term` to be always be an unconstrained inference variable. It is used to\n    /// normalize `alias` as much as possible. In case the alias is rigid - i.e. it cannot\n    /// be normalized in the current environment - this constrains `term` to be equal to\n    /// the alias itself.\n    ///\n    /// It is likely more useful to think of this as a function `normalizes_to(alias)`,\n    /// whose return value is written into `term`.\n    NormalizesTo(ty::NormalizesTo<I>),\n\n    /// Separate from `ClauseKind::Projection` which is used for normalization in new solver.\n    /// This predicate requires two terms to be equal to eachother.\n    ///\n    /// Only used for new solver.\n    AliasRelate(I::Term, I::Term, AliasRelationDirection),\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> Eq for PredicateKind<I> {}\n\n#[derive(Clone, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Debug, Copy)]\n#[cfg_attr(\n AliasRelationDirection::Subtype => write!(f, \"<:\"),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> fmt::Debug for ClauseKind<I> {\n    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\n           PredicateKind::Subtype(pair) => pair.fmt(f),\n            PredicateKind::Coerce(pair) => pair.fmt(f),\n            PredicateKind::DynCompatible(trait_def_id) => {\n                write!(f, tracing::{debug, instrument};\n\nuse self::combine::{PredicateEmittingRelation, super_combine_consts, super_combine_tys};\nuse crate::data_structures::DelayedSet;\npub use crate::relate::*;\nuse crate::solve::Goal;\nuse crate::{self as ty, InferCtxtLike, Interner};\n\npub trait RelateExt: InferCtxtLike {\n    fn relate<T: Relate<Self::Interner>>(\n        &self,\n        param_env: <Self::Interner as Interner>::ParamEnv,\n        lhs: T,\n        variance: ty::Variance,\n        rhs: T,\n        span: <Self::Interner as Interner>::Span,\n    ) -> Result<\n <Self::Interner as Interner>::Predicate>>,\n        TypeError<Self::Interner>,\n    >;\n}\n\nimpl<Infcx: InferCtxtLike> RelateExt for Infcx {\n    fn relate<T: Relate<Self::Interner>>(\n        &self,\n        param_env: <Self::Interner as Interner>::ParamEnv,\n        lhs: T,\n        variance: ty::Variance,\n        rhs: T,\n        span: <Self::Interner as Interner>::Span,\n    ) -> Result<\n        Vec<Goal<Self::Interner, <Self::Interner as Interner>::Predicate>>,\n        TypeError<Self::Interner>,\n    > {\n        let mut relate =\n            SolverRelating::new(self, StructurallyRelateAliases::No, variance, param_env, span);\n        relate.relate(lhs, rhs)?;\n        Ok(relate.goals)\n    }\n\n    fn eq_structurally_relating_aliases<T: Relate<Self::Interner>>(\n        &self,\n        param_env: <Self::Interner as Interner>::ParamEnv,\n        lhs: T,\n        rhs: T,\n        span: <Self::Interner as Interner>::Span,\n    ) -> Result<\n        Vec<Goal<Self::Interner, <Self::Interner as Interner>::Predicate>>,\n        TypeError<Self::Interner>,\n    > {\n        let mut relate = SolverRelating::new(\n            self,\n            StructurallyRelateAliases::Yes,\n            ty::Invariant,\n            param_env,\n            span,\n        );\n        relate.relate(lhs, rhs)?;\n        Ok(relate.goals)\n    }\n}\n\n/// Enforce that `a` is equal to or a subtype of `b`.\npub struct SolverRelating<'infcx, Infcx, I: Interner> {\n    infcx: &'infcx Infcx,\n    // Immutable fields.\n    structurally_relate_aliases: StructurallyRelateAliases,\n    param_env: I::ParamEnv,\n    span: I::Span,\n    // Mutable fields.\n    ambient_variance: ty::Variance,\n    goals: Vec<Goal<I, I::Predicate>>,\n    /// The cache only tracks the `ambient_variance` as it's the\n    /// only field which is mutable and which meaningfully changes\n    /// the result when relating types.\n    ///\n    /// The cache does not track whether the state of the\n    /// `Infcx` has been changed or whether we've added any\n    /// goals to `self.goals`. Whether a goal is added once or multiple\n    /// times is not really meaningful.\n    ///\n    /// Changes in the inference state may delay some type inference to\n    /// the next fulfillment loop. Given that this loop is already\n    /// necessary, this is also not a meaningful change. Consider\n    /// the following three relations:\n    /// ```text\n    /// Vec<?0> sub Vec<?1>\n    /// ?0 eq u32\n    /// Vec<?0> sub Vec<?1>\n    /// ```\n    /// Without a cache, the second `Vec<?0> sub Vec<?1>` would eagerly\n    /// constrain `?1` to `u32`. When using the cache entry from the\n    /// first time we've related these types, this only happens when\n    /// later proving the `Subtype(?0, ?1)` goal from the first relation.\n    cache: DelayedSet<(ty::Variance, I::Ty, I::Ty)>,\n}\n\nimpl<'infcx, Infcx, I> SolverRelating<'infcx, Infcx, I>\nwhere\n    Infcx: InferCtxtLike<Interner = I>,\n    I: Interner,\n{\n    pub fn new(\n        infcx: &'infcx Infcx,\n        structurally_relate_aliases: StructurallyRelateAliases,\n        ambient_variance: ty::Variance,\n        param_env: I::ParamEnv,\n        span: I::Span,\n    ) -> Self {\n        SolverRelating {\n            infcx,\n            structurally_relate_aliases,\n            span,\n            ambient_variance,\n            param_env,\n            goals: vec![],\n            cache: Default::default(),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<Infcx, I> TypeRelation<I> for SolverRelating<'_, Infcx, I>\nwhere\n    Infcx: InferCtxtLike<Interner = I>,\n    I: Interner,\n{\n    fn cx(&self) -> I {\n        self.infcx.cx()\n    }\n\n    fn relate_item_args(\n        &mut self,\n        item_def_id: I::DefId,\n        a_arg: I::GenericArgs,\n        b_arg: I::GenericArgs,\n    ) -> RelateResult<I, I::GenericArgs> {\n        if self.ambient_variance == ty::Invariant {\n            // Avoid fetching the variance if we are in an invariant\n            // context; no need, and it can induce dependency cycles\n            // (e.g., #41849).\n            relate_args_invariantly(self, a_arg, b_arg)\n        } else {\n            let tcx = self.cx();\n            let opt_variances = tcx.variances_of(item_def_id);\n            relate_args_with_variances(self, item_def_id, opt_variances, a_arg, b_arg, false)\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn relate_with_variance<T: Relate<I>>(\n        &mut self,\n        variance: ty::Variance,\n        _info: VarianceDiagInfo<I>,\n        a: T,\n        b: T,\n    ) -> RelateResult<I, T> {\n        let old_ambient_variance = self.ambient_variance;\n        self.ambient_variance = self.ambient_variance.xform(variance);\n        debug!(?self.ambient_variance, \"new ambient variance\");\n\n        let r = if self.ambient_variance == ty::Bivariant { Ok(a) } else { self.relate(a, b) };\n\n        self.ambient_variance = old_ambient_variance;\n        r\n    }\n\n    #[instrument(skip(self), level = \"trace\")]\n    fn tys(&mut self, a: I::Ty, b: I::Ty) -> RelateResult<I, I::Ty> {\n        if a == b {\n            return Ok(a);\n        }\n\n        let infcx = self.infcx;\n        let a = infcx.shallow_resolve(a);\n        let b = infcx.shallow_resolve(b);\n\n        if self.cache.contains(&(self.ambient_variance, a, b)) {\n            return Ok(a);\n        }\n\n        match (a.kind(), b.kind()) {\n            (ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(a_id)), ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(b_id))) => {\n                match self.ambient_variance {\n                    ty::Covariant => {\n                        // can't make progress on `A <: B` if both A and B are\n                        // type variables, so record an obligation.\n                        self.goals.push(Goal::new(\n                            self.cx(),\n                            self.param_env,\n                            ty::Binder::dummy(ty::PredicateKind::Subtype(ty::SubtypePredicate {\n                                a_is_expected: true,\n                                a,\n                                b,\n                            })),\n                        ));\n                    }\n                    ty::Contravariant => {\n                        // can't make progress on `B <: A` if both A and B are\n                        // type variables, so record an obligation.\n                        self.goals.push(Goal::new(\n                            self.cx(),\n                            self.param_env,\n                            ty::Binder::dummy(ty::PredicateKind::Subtype(ty::SubtypePredicate {\n                                a_is_expected: false,\n                                a: b,\n                                b: a,\n                            })),\n                        ));\n                    }\n                    ty::Invariant => {\n                        infcx.equate_ty_vids_raw(a_id, b_id);\n                    }\n                    ty::Bivariant => {\n                        unreachable!(\"Expected bivariance to be handled in relate_with_variance\")\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n\n            (ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(a_vid)), _) => {\n                infcx.instantiate_ty_var_raw(self, true, a_vid, self.ambient_variance, b)?;\n            }\n            (_, ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(b_vid))) => {\n                infcx.instantiate_ty_var_raw(\n                    self,\n                    false,\n                    b_vid,\n                    self.ambient_variance.xform(ty::Contravariant),\n                    a,\n                )?;\n            }\n\n            _ => {\n                super_combine_tys(self.infcx, self, a, b)?;\n            }\n        }\n\n        assert!(self.cache.insert((self.ambient_variance, a, b)));\n\n        Ok(a)\n    }\n\n    #[instrument(skip(self), level = \"trace\")]\n    fn regions(&mut self, a: I::Region, b: I::Region) -> RelateResult<I, I::Region> {\n        match self.ambient_variance {\n            // Subtype(&'a u8, &'b u8) => Outlives('a: 'b) => SubRegion('b, 'a)\n            ty::Covariant => self.infcx.sub_regions(b, a, self.span),\n            // Suptype(&'a u8, &'b u8) => Outlives('b: 'a) => SubRegion('a, 'b)\n            ty::Contravariant => self.infcx.sub_regions(a, b, self.span),\n            ty::Invariant => self.infcx.equate_regions(a, b, self.span),\n            ty::Bivariant => {\n                unreachable!(\"Expected bivariance to be handled in relate_with_variance\")\n            }\n        }\n\n        Ok(a)\n    }\n\n    #[instrument(skip(self), level = \"trace\")]\n    fn consts(&mut self, a: I::Const, b: I::Const) -> RelateResult<I, I::Const> {\n        super_combine_consts(self.infcx, self, a, b)\n    }\n\n    fn binders<T>(\n        &mut self,\n        a: ty::Binder<I, T>,\n        b: ty::Binder<I, T>,\n    ) -> RelateResult<I, ty::Binder<I, T>>\n    where\n        T: Relate<I>,\n    {\n        // If they're equal, then short-circuit.\n        if a == b {\n            return Ok(a);\n        }\n\n        // If they have no bound vars, relate normally.\n        if let Some(a_inner) = a.no_bound_vars()\n            && let Some(b_inner) = b.no_bound_vars()\n        {\n            self.relate(a_inner, b_inner)?;\n            return Ok(a);\n        }\n\n        match self.ambient_variance {\n            // Checks whether `for<..> sub <: for<..> sup` holds.\n            //\n            // For this to hold, **all** instantiations of the super type\n            // have to be a super type of **at least one** instantiation of\n            // the subtype.\n            //\n            // This is implemented by first entering a new universe.\n            // We then replace all bound variables in `sup` with placeholders,\n            // and all bound variables in `sub` with inference vars.\n            // We can then just relate the two resulting types as normal.\n            //\n            // Note: this is a subtle algorithm. For a full explanation, please see\n            // the [rustc dev guide][rd]\n            //\n            // [rd]: https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/borrow_check/region_inference/placeholders_and_universes.html\n            ty::Covariant => {\n                self.infcx.enter_forall(b, |b| {\n                    let a = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(a);\n                    self.relate(a, b)\n                })?;\n            }\n            ty::Contravariant => {\n                self.infcx.enter_forall(a, |a| {\n                    let b = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(b);\n                    self.relate(a, b)\n                })?;\n            }\n\n            // When **equating** binders, we check that there is a 1-to-1\n            // correspondence between the bound vars in both types.\n            //\n            // We do so by separately instantiating one of the binders with\n            // placeholders and the other with inference variables and then\n            // equating the instantiated types.\n            //\n            // We want `for<..> A == for<..> B` -- therefore we want\n            // `exists<..> A == for<..> B` and `exists<..> B == for<..> A`.\n            // Check if `exists<..> A == for<..> B`\n            ty::Invariant => {\n                self.infcx.enter_forall(b, |b| {\n                    let a = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(a);\n                    self.relate(a, b)\n                })?;\n\n                // Check if `exists<..> B == for<..> A`.\n                self.infcx.enter_forall(a, |a| {\n                    let b = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(b);\n                    self.relate(a, b)\n                })?;\n            }\n            ty::Bivariant => {\n                unreachable!(\"Expected bivariance to be handled in relate_with_variance\")\n            }\n        }\n        Ok(a)\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<Infcx, I> PredicateEmittingRelation<Infcx> for SolverRelating<'_, Infcx, I>\nwhere\n    Infcx: InferCtxtLike<Interner = I>,\n    I: Interner,\n{\n    fn span(&self) -> I::Span {\n        Span::dummy()\n    }\n\n    fn param_env(&self) -> I::ParamEnv {\n        self.param_env\n    }\n\n    fn structurally_relate_aliases(&self) -> StructurallyRelateAliases {\n        self.structurally_relate_aliases\n    }\n\n    fn register_predicates(\n        &mut self,\n        obligations: impl IntoIterator<Item: ty::Upcast<I, I::Predicate>>,\n    ) {\n        self.goals.extend(\n            obligations.into_iter().map(|pred| Goal::new(self.infcx.cx(), self.param_env, pred)),\n        );\n    }\n\n    fn register_goals(&mut self, obligations: impl IntoIterator<Item = Goal<I, I::Predicate>>) {\n        self.goals.extend(obligations);\n    }\n\n    fn register_alias_relate_predicate(&mut self, a: I::Ty, b: I::Ty) {\n        self.register_predicates([ty::Binder::dummy(match self.ambient_variance {\n            ty::Covariant => ty::PredicateKind::AliasRelate(\n                a.into(),\n                b.into(),\n                ty::AliasRelationDirection::Subtype,\n            ),\n            // a :> b is b <: a\n            ty::Contravariant => ty::PredicateKind::AliasRelate(\n                b.into(),\n                a.into(),\n                ty::AliasRelationDirection::Subtype,\n            ),\n            ty::Invariant => ty::PredicateKind::AliasRelate(\n                a.into(),\n                b.into(),\n                ty::AliasRelationDirection::Equate,\n            ),\n            ty::Bivariant => {\n                unreachable!(\"Expected bivariance to be handled in relate_with_variance\")\n            }\n        })]);\n    }\n}\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":197,"oldLines":10,"newStart":197,"newLines":10,"lines":["                         self.goals.push(Goal::new(","
