StructurallyRelateAliases::Yes,\n            ty::Invariant,\n            param_env,\n            span,\n        );\n        relate.relate(lhs, rhs)?;\n        Ok(relate.goals)\n    }\n}\n\n/// Enforce that `a` is equal to or a subtype of `b`.\npub struct SolverRelating<'infcx, Infcx, I: Interner> {\n    infcx: &'infcx Infcx,\n    // Immutable fields.\n    structurally_relate_aliases: StructurallyRelateAliases,\n    param_env: I::ParamEnv,\n    span: I::Span,\n    // Mutable fields.\n    ambient_variance: ty::Variance,\n    goals: Vec<Goal<I, I::Predicate>>,\n    /// The cache only tracks the `ambient_variance` as it's the\n    /// only field which is mutable and which meaningfully changes\n    /// the result when relating types.\n    ///\n    /// The cache does not track whether the state of the\n    /// `Infcx` has been changed or whether we've added any\n    /// goals to `self.goals`. Whether a goal is added once or multiple\n    /// times is not really meaningful.\n    ///\n    /// Changes in the inference state may delay some type inference to\n    /// the next fulfillment loop. Given that this loop is already\n    /// necessary, this is also not a meaningful change. Consider\n    /// the following three relations:\n    /// ```text\n    /// Vec<?0> sub Vec<?1>\n    /// ?0 eq u32\n    /// Vec<?0> sub Vec<?1>\n    /// ```\n    /// Without a cache, the second `Vec<?0> sub Vec<?1>` would eagerly\n    /// constrain `?1` to `u32`. When using the cache entry from the\n    /// first time we've related these types, this only happens when\n    /// later proving the `Subtype(?0, ?1)` goal from the first relation.\n    cache: DelayedSet<(ty::Variance, I::Ty, I::Ty)>,\n}\n\nimpl<'infcx, Infcx, I> SolverRelating<'infcx, Infcx, I>\nwhere\n    Infcx: InferCtxtLike<Interner = I>,\n    I: Interner,\n{\n    pub fn new(\n        infcx: &'infcx Infcx,\n        structurally_relate_aliases: StructurallyRelateAliases,\n        ambient_variance: ty::Variance,\n        param_env: I::ParamEnv,\n        span: I::Span,\n    ) -> Self {\n        SolverRelating {\n            infcx,\n            structurally_relate_aliases,\n            span,\n            ambient_variance,\n            param_env,\n            goals: vec![],\n            cache: Default::default(),\n        }\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<Infcx, I> TypeRelation<I> for SolverRelating<'_, Infcx, I>\nwhere\n    Infcx: InferCtxtLike<Interner = I>,\n    I: Interner,\n{\n    fn cx(&self) -> I {\n        self.infcx.cx()\n    }\n\n    fn relate_item_args(\n        &mut self,\n        item_def_id: I::DefId,\n        a_arg: I::GenericArgs,\n        b_arg: I::GenericArgs,\n    ) -> RelateResult<I, I::GenericArgs> {\n        if self.ambient_variance == ty::Invariant {\n            // Avoid fetching the variance if we are in an invariant\n            // context; no need, and it can induce dependency cycles\n            // (e.g., #41849).\n            relate_args_invariantly(self, a_arg, b_arg)\n        } else {\n            let tcx = self.cx();\n            let opt_variances = tcx.variances_of(item_def_id);\n            relate_args_with_variances(self, item_def_id, opt_variances, a_arg, b_arg, false)\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn relate_with_variance<T: Relate<I>>(\n        &mut self,\n        variance: ty::Variance,\n        _info: VarianceDiagInfo<I>,\n        a: T,\n        b: T,\n    ) -> RelateResult<I, T> {\n        let old_ambient_variance = self.ambient_variance;\n        self.ambient_variance = self.ambient_variance.xform(variance);\n        debug!(?self.ambient_variance, \"new ambient variance\");\n\n        let r = if self.ambient_variance == ty::Bivariant { Ok(a) } else { self.relate(a, b) };\n\n        self.ambient_variance = old_ambient_variance;\n        r\n    }\n\n    #[instrument(skip(self), level = \"trace\")]\n    fn tys(&mut self, a: I::Ty, b: I::Ty) -> RelateResult<I, I::Ty> {\n        if a == b {\n            return Ok(a);\n        }\n\n        let infcx = self.infcx;\n        let a = infcx.shallow_resolve(a);\n        let b = infcx.shallow_resolve(b);\n\n        if self.cache.contains(&(self.ambient_variance, a, b)) {\n            return Ok(a);\n        }\n\n        match (a.kind(), b.kind()) {\n            (ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(a_id)), ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(b_id))) => {\n                match self.ambient_variance {\n                    ty::Covariant => {\n                        // can't make progress on `A <: B` if both A and B are\n                        // type variables, so record an obligation.\n                        self.goals.push(Goal::new(\n                            self.cx(),\n                            self.param_env,\n                            ty::Binder::dummy(ty::PredicateKind::Subtype(ty::SubtypeRelation {\n                                a_is_expected: true,\n                                sub_ty: a,\n                                super_ty: b,\n                            })),\n                        ));\n                    }\n                    ty::Contravariant => {\n                        // can't make progress on `B <: A` if both A and B are\n                        // type variables, so record an obligation.\n                        self.goals.push(Goal::new(\n                            self.cx(),\n                            self.param_env,\n                            ty::Binder::dummy(ty::PredicateKind::Subtype(ty::SubtypePredicate {\n                                a_is_expected: false,\n                                a: b,\n                                b: a,\n                            })),\n                        ));\n                    }\n                    ty::Invariant => {\n                        infcx.equate_ty_vids_raw(a_id, b_id);\n                    }\n                    ty::Bivariant => {\n                        unreachable!(\"Expected bivariance to be handled in relate_with_variance\")\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n\n            (ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(a_vid)), _) => {\n                infcx.instantiate_ty_var_raw(self, true, a_vid, self.ambient_variance, b)?;\n            }\n            (_, ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(b_vid))) => {\n                infcx.instantiate_ty_var_raw(\n                    self,\n                    false,\n                    b_vid,\n                    self.ambient_variance.xform(ty::Contravariant),\n                    a,\n                )?;\n            }\n\n            _ => {\n                super_combine_tys(self.infcx, self, a, b)?;\n ty::Covariant => self.infcx.sub_regions(b, a, self.span),\n            // Suptype(&'a u8, &'b u8) => Outlives('b: 'a) => SubRegion('a, 'b)\n            ty::Contravariant => self.infcx.sub_regions(a, b, self.span),\n            ty::Invariant => self.infcx.equate_regions(a, b, self.span),\n            ty::Bivariant => {\n                unreachable!(\"Expected bivariance to be handled in relate_with_variance\")\n            }\n        }\n\n        Ok(a)\n    }\n\n    #[instrument(skip(self), level = \"trace\")]\n    fn consts(&mut self, a: I::Const, b: I::Const) -> RelateResult<I, I::Const> {\n        super_combine_consts(self.infcx, self, a, b)\n    }\n\n    fn binders<T>(\n        &mut self,\n        a: ty::Binder<I, T>,\n        b: ty::Binder<I, T>,\n    ) -> RelateResult<I, ty::Binder<I, T>>\n    where\n        T: Relate<I>,\n    {\n        // If they're equal, then short-circuit.\n        if a == b {\n            return Ok(a);\n        }\n\n        // If they have no bound vars, relate normally.\n        if let Some(a_inner) = a.no_bound_vars()\n            && let Some(b_inner) = b.no_bound_vars()\n        {\n            self.relate(a_inner, b_inner)?;\n            return Ok(a);\n        }\n\n        match self.ambient_variance {\n            // Checks whether `for<..> sub <: for<..> sup` holds.\n            //\n            // For this to hold, **all** instantiations of the super type\n            // have to be a super type of **at least one** instantiation of\n            // the subtype.\n            //\n            // This is implemented by first entering a new universe.\n            // We then replace all bound variables in `sup` with placeholders,\n            // and all bound variables in `sub` with inference vars.\n            // We can then just relate the two resulting types as normal.\n            //\n            // Note: this is a subtle algorithm. For a full explanation, please see\n            // the [rustc dev guide][rd]\n            //\n            // [rd]: https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/borrow_check/region_inference/placeholders_and_universes.html\n            ty::Covariant => {\n                self.infcx.enter_forall(b, |b| {\n                    let a = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(a);\n                    self.relate(a, b)\n                })?;\n            }\n            ty::Contravariant => {\n                self.infcx.enter_forall(a, |a| {\n                    let b = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(b);\n                    self.relate(a, b)\n                })?;\n            }\n\n            // When **equating** binders, we check that there is a 1-to-1\n            // correspondence between the bound vars in both types.\n            //\n            // We do so by separately instantiating one of the binders with\n            // placeholders and the other with inference variables and then\n            // equating the instantiated types.\n            //\n            // We want `for<..> A == for<..> B` -- therefore we want\n            // `exists<..> A == for<..> B` and `exists<..> B == for<..> A`.\n            // Check if `exists<..> A == for<..> B`\n            ty::Invariant => {\n                self.infcx.enter_forall(b, |b| {\n                    let a = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(a);\n                    self.relate(a, b)\n                })?;\n\n                // Check if `exists<..> B == for<..> A`.\n                self.infcx.enter_forall(a, |a| {\n                    let b = self.infcx.instantiate_binder_with_infer(b);\n                    self.relate(a, b)\n                })?;\n            }\n            ty::Bivariant => {\n                unreachable!(\"Expected bivariance to be handled in relate_with_variance\")\n            }\n        }\n Used to determine whether\n        /// inference is required.\n        const HAS_INFER                   = TypeFlags::HAS_TY_INFER.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_RE_INFER.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_CT_INFER.bits();\n\n        /// Does this have `Placeholder`?\n        const HAS_TY_PLACEHOLDER          = 1 = 1 << 8;\n\n        /// Does this have placeholders?\n        const HAS_PLACEHOLDER             = TypeFlags::HAS_TY_PLACEHOLDER.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_RE_PLACEHOLDER.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_CT_PLACEHOLDER.bits();\n\n        /// `true` if there are \"names\" of regions and so forth\n        /// that are local to a particular fn/inferctxt\n        const HAS_FREE_LOCAL_REGIONS      = 1 << 9;\n\n        /// `true` if there are \"names\" of types and regions and so forth\n        /// that are local to a particular fn\n        const HAS_FREE_LOCAL_NAMES        = TypeFlags::HAS_TY_PARAM.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_CT_PARAM.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_TY_INFER.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_CT_INFER.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_TY_PLACEHOLDER.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_CT_PLACEHOLDER.bits()\n                                          // We consider 'freshened' types and constants\n                                          // to depend on a particular fn.\n                                          // The freshening process throws away information,\n                                          // which can make things unsuitable for use in a global\n                                          // cache. Note that there is no 'fresh lifetime' flag -\n                                          // freshening replaces all lifetimes with `ReErased`,\n                                          // which is different from how types/const are freshened.\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_TY_FRESH.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_CT_FRESH.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_FREE_LOCAL_REGIONS.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_RE_ERASED.bits();\n\n        /// Does this have `Projection`?\n        const HAS_TY_PROJECTION           = 1 << 10;\n        /// Does this have `Free` aliases?\n        const HAS_TY_FREE_ALIAS                 = 1 << 11;\n        /// Does this have `Opaque`?\n        const HAS_TY_OPAQUE               = 1 << 12;\n        /// Does this have `Inherent`?\n        const HAS_TY_INHERENT             = 1 << 13;\n        /// Does this have be normalized further?\n        const HAS_ALIAS                   = TypeFlags::HAS_TY_PROJECTION.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_TY_FREE_ALIAS.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_TY_OPAQUE.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_TY_INHERENT.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_CT_PROJECTION.bits();\n\n        /// Is an error type/lifetime/const reachable?\n        const HAS_ERROR                   = 1 << 15;\n\n        /// Does this have any region that \"appears free\" in the type?\n        /// Basically anything but `ReBound` and `ReErased`.\n        const HAS_FREE_REGIONS            = 1 << 16;\n\n        /// Does this have any `ReBound` regions?\n        const HAS_RE_BOUND                = 1 << 17;\n        /// Does this have any `Bound` types?\n        const HAS_TY_BOUND                = 1 << 18;\n        /// Does this have any `ConstKind::Bound` consts?\n        const HAS_CT_BOUND                = 1 << 19;\n        /// Does this have any bound variables?\n        /// Used to check if a global bound is safe to evaluate.\n        const HAS_BOUND_VARS              = TypeFlags::HAS_RE_BOUND.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_TY_BOUND.bits()\n                                          | TypeFlags::HAS_CT_BOUND.bits();\n\n        /// Does this have any `ReErased` regions?\n        const HAS_RE_ERASED               = 1 << 20;\n\n        /// Does this value have parameters/placeholders/inference variables which could be\n        /// replaced later, in a way that would change the results of `impl` specialization?\n        const STILL_FURTHER_SPECIALIZABLE = TypeFlags::HAS_CT_INFER.bits();\n\n        /// Does this value have `InferTy::FreshTy/FreshIntTy/FreshFloatTy`?\n        const HAS_TY_FRESH                = 1 << 21;\n\n        /// Does this value have `InferConst::Fresh`?\n        const HAS_CT_FRESH                = 1 << 22;\n\n        /// Does this have any binders with bound vars (e.g. that need to be anonymized)?\n        const HAS_BINDER_VARS             = 1 << 23;\n\n        /// Does this type have any coroutines in it?\n        const HAS_TY_CORO                 = 1 << 24;\n\n        /// Does this have have a `Bound(BoundVarIndexKind::Canonical, _)`?\n        const HAS_CANONICAL_BOUND      = 1 << 25;\n    }\n}\n\n#[derive(Debug)]\npub struct FlagComputation<I> {\n    pub flags: TypeFlags,\n\n    /// see `Ty::outer_exclusive_binder` for details\n    pub outer_exclusive_binder: ty::DebruijnIndex,\n\n    interner: std::marker::PhantomData<I>,\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> FlagComputation<I> {\n    fn new() -> FlagComputation<I> {\n        FlagComputation {\n            flags: TypeFlags::empty(),\n            outer_exclusive_binder: ty::INNERMOST,\n            interner: std::marker::PhantomData,\n        }\n    }\n\n    #[allow(rustc::usage_of_ty_tykind)]\n    pub fn for_kind(kind: &ty::TyKind<I>) -> FlagComputation<I> {\n        let mut result = FlagComputation::new();\n        result.add_kind(kind);\n        result\n    }\n\n    pub fn for_predicate(binder: ty::Binder<I, ty::PredicateKind<I>>) -> FlagComputation<I> {\n        let mut result = FlagComputation::new();\n        result.add_predicate(binder);\n        result\n    }\n\n    pub fn for_const_kind(kind: &ty::ConstKind<I>) -> FlagComputation<I> {\n        let mut result = FlagComputation::new();\n        result.add_const_kind(kind);\n        result\n    }\n\n    pub fn for_clauses(clauses: &[I::Clause]) -> FlagComputation<I> {\n        let mut result = FlagComputation::new();\n        for c in clauses {\n            result.add_flags(c.as_predicate().flags());\n            result.add_exclusive_binder(c.as_predicate().outer_exclusive_binder());\n        }\n        result\n    }\n\n    fn add_flags(&mut self, flags: TypeFlags) {\n        self.flags = self.flags | flags;\n    }\n\n    /// indicates that `self` refers to something at binding level `binder`\n    fn add_bound_var(&mut self, binder: ty::DebruijnIndex) {\n        let exclusive_binder = binder.shifted_in(1);\n        self.add_exclusive_binder(exclusive_binder);\n    }\n\n    /// indicates that `self` refers to something *inside* binding\n    /// level `binder` -- not bound by `binder`, but bound by the next\n    /// binder internal to it\n    fn add_exclusive_binder(&mut self, exclusive_binder: ty::DebruijnIndex) {\n        self.outer_exclusive_binder = self.outer_exclusive_binder.max(exclusive_binder);\n    }\n\n    /// Adds the flags/depth from a set of types that appear within the current type, but within a\n    /// region binder.\n    fn bound_computation<T, F>(&mut self, value: ty::Binder<I, T>, f: F)\n    where\n        F: FnOnce(&mut Self, T),\n    {\n        let mut computation = FlagComputation::new();\n\n        if !value.bound_vars().is_empty() {\n            computation.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_BINDER_VARS);\n        }\n\n        f(&mut computation, value.skip_binder());\n\n        self.add_flags(computation.flags);\n\n        // The types that contributed to `computation` occurred within\n        // a region binder, so subtract one from the region depth\n        // within when adding the depth to `self`.\n        let outer_exclusive_binder = computation.outer_exclusive_binder;\n        if outer_exclusive_binder > ty::INNERMOST {\n            self.add_exclusive_binder(outer_exclusive_binder.shifted_out(1));\n        } // otherwise, this binder captures nothing\n    }\n\n    #[allow(rustc::usage_of_ty_tykind)]\n    fn add_kind(&mut self, kind: &ty::TyKind<I>) {\n        match *kind {\n            ty::Bool\n            | ty::Char\n            | ty::Int(_)\n            | ty::Float(_)\n            | ty::Uint(_)\n            | ty::Never\n            | ty::Str\n            | ty::Foreign(..) => {}\n\n            ty::Error(_) => self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_ERROR),\n\n            ty::Param(_) => {\n                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_PARAM);\n            }\n\n            ty::Closure(_, args)\n            | ty::CoroutineClosure(_, args)\n            | ty::CoroutineWitness(_, args) => {\n                self.add_args(args.as_slice());\n            }\n\n            ty::Coroutine(_, args) => {\n                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_CORO);\n                self.add_args(args.as_slice());\n            }\n\n            ty::Bound(ty::BoundVarIndexKind::Canonical, _) => {\n                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_BOUND);\n                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_CANONICAL_BOUND);\n            }\n\n            ty::Bound(ty::BoundVarIndexKind::Bound(debruijn), _) => {\n                self.add_bound_var(debruijn);\n                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_BOUND);\n            }\n\n            ty::Placeholder(..) => {\n                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_PLACEHOLDER);\n            }\n\n            ty::Infer(infer) => match infer {\n                ty::FreshTy(_) | ty::FreshIntTy(_) | ty::FreshFloatTy(_) => {\n                    self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_FRESH)\n                }\n\n                ty::TyVar(_) | ty::IntVar(_) | ty::FloatVar(_) => {\n                    self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_TY_INFER)\n                }\n            },\n\n            ty::Adt(_, args) => {\n                self.add_args(args.as_slice());\n            }\n\n            ty::Alias(kind, data) => {\n                self.add_flags(match kind {\n                    ty::Projection => TypeFlags::HAS_TY_PROJECTION,\n                    ty::Free => TypeFlags::HAS_TY_FREE_ALIAS,\n                    ty::Opaque => TypeFlags::HAS_TY_OPAQUE,\n                    ty::Inherent => TypeFlags::HAS_TY_INHERENT,\n                });\n\n                self.add_alias_ty(data);\n            }\n\n            ty::Dynamic(obj, r) => {\n                for predicate in obj.iter() {\n                    self.bound_computation(predicate, |computation, predicate| match predicate {\n                        ty::ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => {\n                            computation.add_args(tr.args.as_slice())\n                        }\n                        ty::ExistentialPredicate::Projection(p) => {\n                            computation.add_existential_projection(&p);\n                        }\n                        ty::ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_) => {}\n                    });\n                }\n\n                self.add_region(r);\n            }\n\n            ty::Array(tt, len) => {\n                self.add_ty(tt);\n                self.add_const(len);\n            }\n\n            ty::Pat(ty, pat) => {\n                self.add_ty(ty);\n                self.add_ty_pat(pat);\n            }\n\n            ty::Slice(tt) => self.add_ty(tt),\n\n            ty::RawPtr(ty, _) => {\n                self.add_ty(ty);\n            }\n\n            ty::Ref(r, ty, _) => {\n                self.add_region(r);\n                self.add_ty(ty);\n            }\n\n            ty::Tuple(types) => {\n                self.add_tys(types);\n            }\n\n            ty::FnDef(_, args) => {\n                self.add_args(args.as_slice());\n            }\n\n            ty::FnPtr(sig_tys, _) => self.bound_computation(sig_tys, |computation, sig_tys| {\n                computation.add_tys(sig_tys.inputs_and_output);\n            }),\n\n            ty::UnsafeBinder(bound_ty) => {\n                self.bound_computation(bound_ty.into(), |computation, ty| {\n                    computation.add_ty(ty);\n                })\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn add_ty_pat(&mut self, pat: <I as Interner>::Pat) {\n        self.add_flags(pat.flags());\n        self.add_exclusive_binder(pat.outer_exclusive_binder());\n    }\n\n    fn add_predicate(&mut self, binder: ty::Binder<I, ty::PredicateKind<I>>) {\n        self.bound_computation(binder, |computation, atom| computation.add_predicate_atom(atom));\n    }\n\n    fn add_predicate_atom(&mut self, atom: ty::PredicateKind<I>) {\n        match atom {\n            ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::Trait(trait_pred)) => {\n                self.add_args(trait_pred.trait_ref.args.as_slice());\n            }\n            ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::HostEffect(ty::HostEffectPredicate {\n                trait_ref,\n                constness: _,\n            })) => {\n                self.add_args(trait_ref.args.as_slice());\n            }\n            ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::RegionOutlives(ty::OutlivesPredicate(\n                a,\n                b,\n            ))) => {\n                self.add_region(a);\n                self.add_region(b);\n            }\n            ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::TypeOutlives(ty::OutlivesPredicate(\n                ty,\n                region,\n            ))) => {\n                self.add_ty(ty);\n                self.add_region(region);\n            }\n ty::PredicateKind::Subtype(ty::SubtypePredicate { a_is_expected: _, a, b }) => {\n                self.add_ty(a);\n                self.add_ty(b);\n            }\n            ty::PredicateKind::Coerce(ty::CoercePredicate { a, b }) => {\n                self.add_ty(a);\n                self.add_ty(b);\n            }\n            ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::Projection(ty::ProjectionPredicate {\n                projection_term,\n                term,\n            })) => {\n ty::PredicateKind::AliasRelate(t1, t2, _) => {\n                self.add_term(t1);\n                self.add_term(t2);\n            }\n            ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::UnstableFeature(_sym)) => {}\n            ty::PredicateKind::Ambiguous => {}\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn add_ty(&mut self, ty: I::Ty) {\n        self.add_flags(ty.flags());\n        self.add_exclusive_binder(ty.outer_exclusive_binder());\n    }\n\n    fn add_tys(&mut self, tys: I::Tys) {\n        for ty in tys.iter() {\n            self.add_ty(ty);\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn add_region(&mut self, r: I::Region) {\n        self.add_flags(r.flags());\n        if let ty::ReBound(ty::BoundVarIndexKind::Bound(debruijn), _) = r.kind() {\n            self.add_bound_var(debruijn);\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn add_const(&mut self, c: I::Const) {\n        self.add_flags(c.flags());\n        self.add_exclusive_binder(c.outer_exclusive_binder());\n    }\n\n    fn add_const_kind(&mut self, c: &ty::ConstKind<I>) {\n        match *c {\n            ty::ConstKind::Unevaluated(uv) => {\n                self.add_args(uv.args.as_slice());\n                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_CT_PROJECTION);\n            }\n            ty::ConstKind::Infer(infer) => match infer {\n                ty::InferConst::Fresh(_) => self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_CT_FRESH),\n                ty::InferConst::Var(_) => self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_CT_INFER),\n            },\n            ty::ConstKind::Bound(ty::BoundVarIndexKind::Bound(debruijn), _) => {\n                self.add_bound_var(debruijn);\n                self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_CT_BOUND);\n            }\n self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_CT_PLACEHOLDER);\n            }\n            ty::ConstKind::Value(cv) => self.add_ty(cv.ty()),\n            ty::ConstKind::Expr(e) => self.add_args(e.args().as_slice()),\n            ty::ConstKind::Error(_) => self.add_flags(TypeFlags::HAS_ERROR),\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn add_existential_projection(&mut self, projection: &ty::ExistentialProjection<I>) {\n        self.add_args(projection.args.as_slice());\n        match projection.term.kind() {\n            ty::TermKind::Ty(ty) => self.add_ty(ty),\n            ty::TermKind::Const(ct) => self.add_const(ct),\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn add_alias_ty(&mut self, alias_ty: ty::AliasTy<I>) {\n        self.add_args(alias_ty.args.as_slice());\n    }\n\n    fn {\n            match arg.kind() {\n                ty::GenericArgKind::Type(ty) => self.add_ty(ty),\n                ty::GenericArgKind::Lifetime(lt) => self.add_region(lt),\n                ty::GenericArgKind::Const(ct) => self.add_const(ct),\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn add_term(&mut self, term: I::Term) {\n        match term.kind() {\n            ty::TermKind::Ty(ty) => self.add_ty(ty),\n            ty::TermKind::Const(ct) => self.add_const(ct),\n        }\n    }\n}\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":391,"oldLines":9,"newStart":391,"newLines":9,"lines":[" Imagine you have a function `F: FnOnce(&[T]) -> R`, plus an iterator `iter`\n/// that produces `T` items. You could combine them with\n/// `f(&iter.collect::<Vec<_>>())`, but this requires allocating memory for the\n/// `Vec`.\n///\n/// This trait allows for faster implementations, intended for cases where the\n/// number of items produced by the iterator is small. There is a blanket impl\n/// for `T` items, but there is also a fallible impl for `Result<T, E>` items.\npub trait CollectAndApply<T, R>: Sized {\n    type Output;\n\n    /// Produce a result of type `Self::Output` from `iter`. The result will\n    /// typically be produced by applying `f` on the elements produced by\n    /// `iter`, though this may not happen in some impls, e.g. if an error\n    /// occurred during iteration.\n    fn collect_and_apply<I, F>(iter: I, f: F) -> Self::Output\n    where\n        I: Iterator<Item = Self>,\n        F: FnOnce(&[T]) -> R;\n}\n\n/// The blanket impl that always collects all elements and applies `f`.\nimpl<T, R> CollectAndApply<T, R> for T {\n    type Output = R;\n\n    /// Equivalent to `f(&iter.collect::<Vec<_>>())`.\n    fn collect_and_apply<I, F>(mut iter: I, f: F) -> R\n    where\n        I: Iterator<Item = T>,\n        F: FnOnce(&[T]) -> R,\n    {\n        // This code is hot enough that it's worth specializing for the most\n        // common length lists, to avoid the overhead of `Vec` creation.\n\n        let Some(t0) = iter.next() else {\n            return f(&[]);\n        };\n\n        let Some(t1) = iter.next() else {\n            return f(&[t0]);\n        };\n\n        let Some(t2) = iter.next() else {\n            return f(&[t0, t1]);\n        };\n\n        let Some(t3) = iter.next() else {\n            return f(&[t0, t1, t2]);\n        };\n\n        let Some(t4) = iter.next() else {\n            return f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3]);\n        };\n\n        let Some(t5) = iter.next() else {\n            return f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4]);\n        };\n\n        let Some(t6) = iter.next() else {\n            return f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4, t5]);\n        };\n\n        let Some(t7) = iter.next() else {\n            return f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4, t5, t6]);\n        };\n\n        let Some(t8) = iter.next() else {\n            return f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4, t5, t6, t7]);\n        };\n\n        f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4, t5, t6, t7, t8].into_iter().chain(iter).collect::<Vec<_>>())\n    }\n}\n\n/// A fallible impl that will fail, without calling `f`, if there are any\n/// errors during collection.\nimpl<T, R, E> CollectAndApply<T, R> for Result<T, E> {\n    type Output = Result<R, E>;\n\n    /// Equivalent to `Ok(f(&iter.collect::<Result<Vec<_>>>()?))`.\n    fn collect_and_apply<I, F>(mut iter: I, f: F) -> Result<R, E>\n    where\n        I: Iterator<Item = Result<T, E>>,\n        F: FnOnce(&[T]) -> R,\n    {\n        // This code is hot enough that it's worth specializing for the most\n        // common length lists, to avoid the overhead of `Vec` creation.\n\n        let Some(t0) = iter.next() else {\n            return Ok(f(&[]));\n        };\n        let t0 = t0?;\n\n        let Some(t1) = iter.next() else {\n            return Ok(f(&[t0]));\n        };\n        let t1 = t1?;\n\n        let Some(t2) = iter.next() else {\n            return Ok(f(&[t0, t1]));\n        };\n        let t2 = t2?;\n\n        let Some(t3) = iter.next() else {\n            return Ok(f(&[t0, t1, t2]));\n        };\n        let t3 = t3?;\n\n        let Some(t4) = iter.next() else {\n            return Ok(f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3]));\n        };\n        let t4 = t4?;\n\n        let Some(t5) = iter.next() else {\n            return Ok(f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4]));\n        };\n        let t5 = t5?;\n\n        let Some(t6) = iter.next() else {\n            return Ok(f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4, t5]));\n        };\n        let t6 = t6?;\n\n        let Some(t7) = iter.next() else {\n            return Ok(f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4, t5, t6]));\n        };\n        let t7 = t7?;\n\n        let Some(t8) = iter.next() else {\n            return Ok(f(&[t0, t1, t2, t3, t4, t5, t6, t7]));\n        };\n        let t8 = t8?;\n\n        Ok(f(&[Ok(t0), Ok(t1), Ok(t2), Ok(t3), Ok(t4), Ok(t5), Ok(t6), Ok(t7), Ok(t8)]\n            .into_iter()\n            .chain(iter)\n            .collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>()?))\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<I: Interner> search_graph::Cx for I {\n    type Input = CanonicalInput<I>;\n    type Result = QueryResult<I>;\n    type AmbiguityInfo = Certainty;\n\n    type DepNodeIndex = I::DepNodeIndex;\n    type Tracked<T: rustc_type_ir::inherent::Clauses<TyCtxt<'tcx>> for ty::Clauses<'tcx> {}\n\n#[extension(pub trait ExistentialPredicateStableCmpExt<'tcx>)]\nimpl<'tcx> ExistentialPredicate<'tcx> {\n    /// Compares via an ordering that will not change if modules are reordered or other changes are\n    /// made to the tree. In particular, this ordering is preserved across incremental compilations.\n    fn stable_cmp(&self, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>, other: &Self) -> Ordering {\n        match (*self, *other) {\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => Ordering::Equal,\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref b)) => {\n                tcx.def_path_hash(a.def_id).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(b.def_id))\n            }\n            (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref b)) => {\n                tcx.def_path_hash(*a).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(*b))\n            }\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => {\n                Ordering::Greater\n            }\n            (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\n            (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_), _) => Ordering::Greater,\n        }\n    }\n}\n\npub type PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>;\n\nimpl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::BoundExistentialPredicates<TyCtxt<'tcx>>\n    for &'tcx ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>\n{\n    fn principal_def_id(self) -> Option<DefId> {\n        self.principal_def_id()\n    }\n\n    fn principal(self) -> Option<ty::PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>> {\n        self.principal()\n    }\n\n    fn auto_traits(self) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = DefId> {\n        self.auto_traits()\n    }\n\n    fn projection_bounds(\n        self,\n    ) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\n        self.projection_bounds()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\n    /// Returns the \"principal `DefId`\" of this set of existential predicates.\n    ///\n    /// A Rust trait object type consists (in addition to a lifetime bound)\n    /// of a set of trait bounds, which are separated into any number\n    /// of auto-trait bounds, and at most one non-auto-trait bound. The\n    /// non-auto-trait bound is called the \"principal\" of the trait\n    /// object.\n    ///\n    /// Only the principal can have methods or type parameters (because\n    /// auto traits can have neither of them). This is important, because\n    /// it means the auto traits can be treated as an unordered set (methods\n    /// would force an order for the vtable, while relating traits with\n    /// type parameters without knowing the order to relate them in is\n    /// a rather non-trivial task).\n    ///\n    /// For example, in the trait object `dyn std::fmt::Debug + Sync`, the\n    /// principal bound is `Some(std::fmt::Debug)`, while the auto-trait bounds\n    /// are the set `{Sync}`.\n    ///\n    /// It is also possible to have a \"trivial\" trait object that\n    /// consists only of auto traits, with no principal - for example,\n    /// `dyn Send + Sync`. In that case, the set of auto-trait bounds\n    /// is `{Send, Sync}`, while there is no principal. These trait objects\n    /// have a \"trivial\" vtable consisting of just the size, alignment,\n    /// and destructor.\n    pub fn principal(&self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>> {\n        self[0]\n            .map_bound(|this| match this {\n                ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => Some(tr),\n                _ => None,\n            })\n            .transpose()\n    }\n\n    pub fn principal_def_id(&self) -> Option<DefId> {\n        self.principal().map(|trait_ref| trait_ref.skip_binder().def_id)\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn projection_bounds(\n        &self,\n    ) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\n        self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| {\n            predicate\n                .map_bound(|pred| match pred {\n                    ExistentialPredicate::Projection(projection) => Some(projection),\n                    _ => None,\n                })\n                .transpose()\n        })\n    }\n\n    #[inline]\n    pub fn auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = DefId> {\n        self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| match predicate.skip_binder() {\n            ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(did) => Some(did),\n            _ => None,\n        })\n    }\n\n    pub fn without_auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\n        self.iter().filter(|predicate| {\n            !matches!(predicate.as_ref().skip_binder(), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_))\n        })\n    }\n}\n\npub type PolyTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitRef<'tcx>>;\npub type PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>;\npub type PolyExistentialProjection<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>;\n\nimpl<'tcx> Clause<'tcx> {\n    /// Performs a instantiation suitable for going from a\n    /// poly-trait-ref to supertraits that must hold if that\n    /// poly-trait-ref holds. This is slightly different from a normal\n    /// instantiation in terms of what happens with bound regions. See\n    /// lengthy comment below for details.\n    pub fn instantiate_supertrait(\n        self,\n        tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>,\n        trait_ref: ty::PolyTraitRef<'tcx>,\n    ) -> Clause<'tcx> {\n        // The interaction between HRTB and supertraits is not entirely\n        // obvious. Let me walk you (and myself) through an example.\n        //\n        // Let's start with an easy case. Consider two traits:\n        //\n        //     trait Foo<'a>: Bar<'a,'a> { }\n        //     trait Bar<'b,'c> { }\n        //\n        // Now, if we have a trait reference `for<'x> T: Foo<'x>`, then\n        // we can deduce that `for<'x> T: Bar<'x,'x>`. Basically, if we\n        // knew that `Foo<'x>` (for any 'x) then we also know that\n        // `Bar<'x,'x>` (for any 'x). This more-or-less falls out from\n        // normal instantiation.\n        //\n        // In terms of why this is sound, the idea is that whenever there\n        // is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>`, it must show that `T:Bar<'a,'a>`\n        // holds. So if there is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>` that applies to\n        // all `'a`, then we must know that `T:Bar<'a,'a>` holds for all\n        // `'a`.\n        //\n        // Another example to be careful of is this:\n        //\n        //     trait Foo1<'a>: for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b> { }\n        //     trait Bar1<'b,'c> { }\n        //\n        // Here, if we have `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`, then what do we know?\n        // The answer is that we know `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`. The\n        // reason is similar to the previous example: any impl of\n        // `T:Foo1<'x>` must show that `for<'b> T: Bar1<'x, 'b>`. So\n        // basically we would want to collapse the bound lifetimes from\n        // the input (`trait_ref`) and the supertraits.\n        //\n        // To achieve this in practice is fairly straightforward. Let's\n        // consider the more complicated scenario:\n        //\n        // - We start out with `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`. In this case, `'x`\n        //   has a De Bruijn index of 1. We want to produce `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`,\n        //   where both `'x` and `'b` would have a DB index of 1.\n        //   The instantiation from the input trait-ref is therefore going to be\n        //   `'a => 'x` (where `'x` has a DB index of 1).\n        // - The supertrait-ref is `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, where `'a` is an\n        //   early-bound parameter and `'b` is a late-bound parameter with a\n        //   DB index of 1.\n        // - If we replace `'a` with `'x` from the input, it too will have\n        //   a DB index of 1, and thus we'll have `for<'x,'b> Bar1<'x,'b>`\n        //   just as we wanted.\n        //\n        // There is only one catch. If we just apply the instantiation `'a\n        // => 'x` to `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, the instantiation code will\n        // adjust the DB index because we instantiating into a binder (it\n        // tries to be so smart...) resulting in `for<'x> for<'b>\n        // Bar1<'x,'b>` (we have no syntax for this, so use your\n        // imagination). Basically the 'x will have DB index of 2 and 'b\n        // will have DB index of 1. Not quite what we want. So we apply\n        // the instantiation to the *contents* of the trait reference,\n        // rather than the trait reference itself (put another way, the\n        // instantiation code expects equal binding levels in the values\n        // from the instantiation and the value being instantiated into, and\n        // this trick achieves that).\n\n        // Working through the second example:\n        // trait_ref: for<'x> T: Foo1<'^0.0>; args: [T, '^0.0]\n        // predicate: for<'b> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0>; args: [Self, 'a, '^0.0]\n        // We want to end up with:\n        //     for<'x, 'b> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\n        // To do this:\n        // 1) We must shift all bound vars in predicate by the length\n        //    of trait ref's bound vars. So, we would end up with predicate like\n        //    Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\n        // 2) We can then apply the trait args to this, ending up with\n        //    T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\n        // 3) Finally, to create the final bound vars, we concatenate the bound\n        //    vars of the trait ref with those of the predicate:\n        //    ['x, 'b]\n        let bound_pred = self.kind();\n        let pred_bound_vars = bound_pred.bound_vars();\n        let trait_bound_vars = trait_ref.bound_vars();\n        // 1) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0> -> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\n        let shifted_pred =\n            tcx.shift_bound_var_indices(trait_bound_vars.len(), bound_pred.skip_binder());\n        // 2) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1> -> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\n        let new = EarlyBinder::bind(shifted_pred).instantiate(tcx, trait_ref.skip_binder().args);\n        // 3) ['x] + ['b] -> ['x, 'b]\n        let bound_vars =\n            tcx.mk_bound_variable_kinds_from_iter(trait_bound_vars.iter().chain(pred_bound_vars));\n\n        // FIXME: Is it really perf sensitive to use reuse_or_mk_predicate here?\n        tcx.reuse_or_mk_predicate(\n            self.as_predicate(),\n            ty::Binder::bind_with_vars(PredicateKind::Clause(new), bound_vars),\n        )\n        .expect_clause()\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, PredicateKind<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\n    fn upcast_from(from: PredicateKind<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\n incompletely guide inference via ty var\n                // self types.\n                // FIXME: Properly consider opaques here.\n                && self.inner.borrow_mut().opaque_types().is_empty()\n            {\n                return Some(Certainty::AMBIGUOUS);\n            }\n\n            if trait_pred.polarity() == ty::PredicatePolarity::Positive {\n                match self.0.tcx.as_lang_item(trait_pred.def_id()) {\n                    Some(LangItem::Sized) | Some(LangItem::MetaSized) => {\n                        let predicate = self.resolve_vars_if_possible(goal.predicate);\n                        if sizedness_fast_path(self.tcx, predicate, goal.param_env) {\n                            return Some(Certainty::Yes);\n                        }\n                    }\n                    Some(LangItem::Copy | LangItem::Clone) => {\n                        let self_ty =\n                            self.resolve_vars_if_possible(trait_pred.self_ty().skip_binder());\n                        // Unlike `Sized` traits, which always prefer the built-in impl,\n                        // `Copy`/`Clone` may be shadowed by a param-env candidate which\n                        // could force a lifetime error or guide inference. While that's\n                        // not generally desirable, it is observable, so for now let's\n                        // ignore this fast path for types that have regions or infer.\n }\n\n        let pred = goal.predicate.kind();\n        match pred.no_bound_vars()? {\n            ty::PredicateKind::DynCompatible(def_id) if self.0.tcx.is_dyn_compatible(def_id) => {\n SubregionOrigin::RelateRegionParamBound(span, None),\n                    outlives.1,\n                    outlives.0,\n                );\n                Some(Certainty::Yes)\n            }\n trait_assoc_def_id)?;\n\n        let eligible = if node_item.is_final() {\n            // Non-specializable items are always projectable.\n            true\n        } else {\n            // Only We probably need to register >1 since we may have an OR of ANDs.\n    fn is_transmutable(\n        &self,\n        dst: Ty<'tcx>,\n        src: Ty<'tcx>,\n        assume: ty::Const<'tcx>,\n    ) -> Result<Certainty, NoSolution> {\n        // Erase regions because we compute layouts in `rustc_transmute`,\n        // which will ICE for region vars.\n        let (dst, src) = self.tcx.erase_and_anonymize_regions((dst, src));\n\n        let Some(assume) = rustc_transmute::Assume::from_const(self.tcx, assume) else {\n            return Err(NoSolution);\n        };\n\n        // FIXME(transmutability): This really should be returning nested goals for `Answer::If*`\n        match rustc_transmute::TransmuteTypeEnv::new(self.0.tcx)\n            .is_transmutable(rustc_transmute::Types { src, dst }, assume)\n        {\n            rustc_transmute::Answer::Yes => Ok(Certainty::Yes),\n            rustc_transmute::Answer::No(_) | rustc_transmute::Answer::If(_) => Err(NoSolution),\n        }\n    }\n}\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":124,"oldLines":9,"newStart":124,"newLines":9,"lines":[" ","                 Some(Certainty::Yes)","             }","-            ty::PredicateKind::Subtype(ty::SubtypePredicate { a, b, .. })","+            ty::PredicateKind::Subtype(ty::SubtypeRelation { sub_ty, super_ty, .. })","             | ty::PredicateKind::Coerce(ty::CoercePredicate { a, b }) => {","-                match (self.shallow_resolve(a).kind(), self.shallow_resolve(b).kind()) {","+                match (self.shallow_resolve(sub_ty).kind(), self.shallow_resolve(super_ty).kind()) {","                     (&ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(a_vid)), &ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(b_vid))) => {","                         self.sub_unify_ty_vids_raw(a_vid, b_vid);","                         Some(Certainty::AMBIGUOUS)"]}],"userModified":false,"replaceAll":false}}

sizedness_fast_path};\n\npub(crate) type PendingPredicateObligations<'tcx> = ThinVec<PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx>>;\n\nimpl<'tcx> ForestObligation for PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx> {\n    /// Note that we include both the `ParamEnv` and the `Predicate`,\n    /// as the `ParamEnv` can influence whether fulfillment succeeds\n    /// or fails.\n    type CacheKey = ty::ParamEnvAnd<'tcx, ty::Predicate<'tcx>>;\n\n    fn as_cache_key(&self) -> Self::CacheKey {\n        self.obligation.param_env.and(self.obligation.predicate)\n    }\n}\n\n/// The fulfillment context is used to drive trait resolution. It\n/// consists of a list of obligations that must be (eventually)\n/// satisfied. The job is to track which are satisfied, which yielded\n/// errors, and which are still pending. At any point, users can call\n/// `try_evaluate_obligations`, and the fulfillment context will try to do\n/// selection, retaining only those obligations that remain\n/// ambiguous. This may be helpful in pushing type inference\n/// along. Once all type inference constraints have been generated, the\n/// method `evaluate_obligations_error_on_ambiguity` can be used to report any remaining\n/// ambiguous cases as errors.\npub struct FulfillmentContext<'tcx, E: 'tcx> {\n    /// A list of all obligations that have been registered with this\n    /// fulfillment context.\n    predicates: ObligationForest<PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx>>,\n\n    /// The snapshot in which this context was created. Using the context\n    /// outside of this snapshot leads to subtle bugs if the snapshot\n    /// gets rolled back. Because of this we explicitly check that we only\n    /// use the context in exactly this snapshot.\n    usable_in_snapshot: usize,\n\n    _errors: PhantomData<E>,\n}\n\n#[derive(Clone, Debug)]\npub struct PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx> {\n    pub obligation: PredicateObligation<'tcx>,\n    // This is far more often read than modified, meaning that we\n    // should mostly optimize for reading speed, while modifying is not as relevant.\n    //\n    // For whatever reason using a boxed slice is slower than using a `Vec` here.\n    pub stalled_on: Vec<TyOrConstInferVar>,\n}\n\n// `PendingPredicateObligation` is used a lot. Make sure it doesn't unintentionally get bigger.\n#[cfg(target_pointer_width = \"64\")]\nrustc_data_structures::static_assert_size!(PendingPredicateObligation<'_>, 72);\n\nimpl<'tcx, E> FulfillmentContext<'tcx, E>\nwhere\n    E: FromSolverError<'tcx, OldSolverError<'tcx>>,\n{\n    /// Creates a new fulfillment context.\n    pub(super) fn new(infcx: &InferCtxt<'tcx>) -> FulfillmentContext<'tcx, E> {\n        assert!(\n            !infcx.next_trait_solver(),\n            \"old trait solver fulfillment context created when \\\n            infcx is set up for new trait solver\"\n        );\n        FulfillmentContext {\n            predicates: ObligationForest::new(),\n            usable_in_snapshot: infcx.num_open_snapshots(),\n            _errors: PhantomData,\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Attempts to select obligations using `selcx`.\n    fn select(&mut self, selcx: SelectionContext<'_, 'tcx>) -> Vec<E> {\n        let span = debug_span!(\"select\", obligation_forest_size = ?self.predicates.len());\n        let _enter = span.enter();\n        let infcx = selcx.infcx;\n\n        // Process pending obligations.\n        let outcome: Outcome<_, _> =\n            self.predicates.process_obligations(&mut FulfillProcessor { selcx });\n\n        // FIXME: if we kept the original cache key, we could mark projection\n        // obligations as complete for the projection cache here.\n\n        let errors: Vec<E> = outcome\n            .errors\n            .into_iter()\n            .map(|err| E::from_solver_error(infcx, OldSolverError(err)))\n            .collect();\n\n        debug!(\n            \"select({} predicates remaining, {} errors) done\",\n            self.predicates.len(),\n            errors.len()\n        );\n\n        errors\n    }\n}\n\nimpl<'tcx, E> TraitEngine<'tcx, E> for FulfillmentContext<'tcx, E>\nwhere\n    E: FromSolverError<'tcx, OldSolverError<'tcx>>,\n{\n    #[inline]\n    fn register_predicate_obligation(\n        &mut self,\n        infcx: &InferCtxt<'tcx>,\n        mut obligation: PredicateObligation<'tcx>,\n    ) {\n        assert_eq!(self.usable_in_snapshot, infcx.num_open_snapshots());\n        // this helps to reduce duplicate errors, as well as making\n        // debug output much nicer to read and so on.\n        debug_assert!(!obligation.param_env.has_non_region_infer());\n        obligation.predicate = infcx.resolve_vars_if_possible(obligation.predicate);\n\n        debug!(?obligation, \"register_predicate_obligation\");\n\n        self.predicates\n            .register_obligation(PendingPredicateObligation { obligation, stalled_on: vec![] });\n    }\n\n    fn collect_remaining_errors(&mut self, infcx: &InferCtxt<'tcx>) -> Vec<E> {\n        self.predicates\n            .to_errors(FulfillmentErrorCode::Ambiguity { overflow: None })\n            .into_iter()\n            .map(|err| E::from_solver_error(infcx, OldSolverError(err)))\n            .collect()\n    }\n\n    fn try_evaluate_obligations(&mut self, infcx: &InferCtxt<'tcx>) -> Vec<E> {\n        let selcx = SelectionContext::new(infcx);\n        self.select(selcx)\n    }\n\n    fn drain_stalled_obligations_for_coroutines(\n        &mut self,\n        infcx: &InferCtxt<'tcx>,\n    ) -> PredicateObligations<'tcx> {\n        let stalled_coroutines = match infcx.typing_mode() {\n            TypingMode::Analysis { defining_opaque_types_and_generators } => {\n                defining_opaque_types_and_generators\n            }\n            TypingMode::Coherence\n            | TypingMode::Borrowck { defining_opaque_types: _ }\n            | TypingMode::PostBorrowckAnalysis { defined_opaque_types: _ }\n            | TypingMode::PostAnalysis => return Default::default(),\n        };\n\n        if stalled_coroutines.is_empty() {\n            return Default::default();\n        }\n\n        let mut processor = DrainProcessor {\n            infcx,\n            removed_predicates: PredicateObligations::new(),\n            stalled_coroutines,\n        };\n        let outcome: Outcome<_, _> = self.predicates.process_obligations(&mut processor);\n        assert!(outcome.errors.is_empty());\n        return processor.removed_predicates;\n\n        struct DrainProcessor<'a, 'tcx> {\n            infcx: &'a InferCtxt<'tcx>,\n            removed_predicates: PredicateObligations<'tcx>,\n            stalled_coroutines: &'tcx ty::List<LocalDefId>,\n        }\n\n        impl<'tcx> ObligationProcessor for DrainProcessor<'_, 'tcx> {\n            type Obligation = PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx>;\n            type Error = !;\n            type OUT = Outcome<Self::Obligation, Self::Error>;\n\n            fn needs_process_obligation(&self, pending_obligation: &Self::Obligation) -> bool {\n                self.infcx\n                    .resolve_vars_if_possible(pending_obligation.obligation.predicate)\n                    .visit_with(&mut StalledOnCoroutines {\n                        stalled_coroutines: self.stalled_coroutines,\n                        span: DUMMY_SP,\n                        cache: Default::default(),\n                    })\n                    .is_break()\n            }\n\n            fn process_obligation(\n                &mut self,\n                pending_obligation: &mut PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx>,\n            ) -> ProcessResult<PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx>, !> {\n                assert!(self.needs_process_obligation(pending_obligation));\n                self.removed_predicates.push(pending_obligation.obligation.clone());\n                ProcessResult::Changed(Default::default())\n            }\n\n            fn process_backedge<'c, I>(\n                &mut self,\n                cycle: I,\n                _marker: PhantomData<&'c PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx>>,\n            ) -> Result<(), !>\n            where\n                I: Clone + Iterator<Item = &'c PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx>>,\n            {\n                self.removed_predicates.extend(cycle.map(|c| c.obligation.clone()));\n                Ok(())\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    fn has_pending_obligations(&self) -> bool {\n        self.predicates.has_pending_obligations()\n    }\n\n    fn pending_obligations(&self) -> PredicateObligations<'tcx> {\n        self.predicates.map_pending_obligations(|o| o.obligation.clone())\n    }\n}\n\nstruct FulfillProcessor<'a, 'tcx> {\n    selcx: SelectionContext<'a, 'tcx>,\n}\n\nfn mk_pending<'tcx>(\n    parent: &PredicateObligation<'tcx>,\n    os: PredicateObligations<'tcx>,\n) -> PendingPredicateObligations<'tcx> {\n    os.into_iter()\n        .map(|mut o| {\n            o.set_depth_from_parent(parent.recursion_depth);\n            PendingPredicateObligation { obligation: o, stalled_on: vec![] }\n        })\n        .collect()\n}\n\nimpl<'a, 'tcx> ObligationProcessor for FulfillProcessor<'a, 'tcx> {\n    type Obligation = PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx>;\n    type Error = FulfillmentErrorCode<'tcx>;\n    type OUT = Outcome<Self::Obligation, Self::Error>;\n\n    /// Compared to `needs_process_obligation` this and its callees\n    /// contain some optimizations that come at the price of false negatives.\n    ///\n    /// They\n    /// - reduce branching by covering only the most common case\n    /// - take a read-only view of the unification tables which allows skipping undo_log\n    ///   construction.\n    /// - bail out on value-cache misses in ena to avoid pointer chasing\n    /// - hoist RefCell locking out of the loop\n    #[inline]\n    fn skippable_obligations<'b>(\n        &'b self,\n        it: impl Iterator<Item = &'b Self::Obligation>,\n    ) -> usize {\n        let is_unchanged = self.selcx.infcx.is_ty_infer_var_definitely_unchanged();\n\n        it.take_while(|o| match o.stalled_on.as_slice() {\n            [o] => is_unchanged(*o),\n            _ => false,\n        })\n        .count()\n    }\n\n    /// Identifies whether a predicate obligation needs processing.\n    ///\n    /// This is always inlined because it has a single callsite and it is\n    /// called *very* frequently. Be careful modifying this code! Several\n    /// compile-time benchmarks are very sensitive to even small changes.\n    #[inline(always)]\n    fn needs_process_obligation(&self, pending_obligation: &Self::Obligation) -> bool {\n        // If we were stalled on some unresolved variables, first check whether\n        // any of them have been resolved; if not, don't bother doing more work\n        // yet.\n        let stalled_on = &pending_obligation.stalled_on;\n        match stalled_on.len() {\n            // This case is the hottest most of the time, being hit up to 99%\n            // of the time. `keccak` and `cranelift-codegen-0.82.1` are\n            // benchmarks that particularly stress this path.\n            1 => self.selcx.infcx.ty_or_const_infer_var_changed(stalled_on[0]),\n\n            // In this case we haven't changed, but wish to make a change. Note\n            // that this is a special case, and is not equivalent to the `_`\n            // case below, which would return `false` for an empty `stalled_on`\n            // vector.\n            //\n            // This case is usually hit only 1% of the time or less, though it\n            // reaches 20% in `wasmparser-0.101.0`.\n            0 => true,\n\n            // This case is usually hit only 1% of the time or less, though it\n            // reaches 95% in `mime-0.3.16`, 64% in `wast-54.0.0`, and 12% in\n            // `inflate-0.4.5`.\n            //\n            // The obvious way of writing this, with a call to `any()` and no\n            // closure, is currently slower than this version.\n            _ => (|| {\n                for &infer_var in stalled_on {\n                    if self.selcx.infcx.ty_or_const_infer_var_changed(infer_var) {\n                        return true;\n                    }\n                }\n                false\n            })(),\n        }\n    }\n\n    /// Processes a predicate obligation and returns either:\n    /// - `Changed(v)` if the predicate is true, presuming that `v` are also true\n    /// - `Unchanged` if we don't have enough info to be sure\n    /// - `Error(e)` if the predicate does not hold\n    ///\n    /// This is called much less often than `needs_process_obligation`, so we\n    /// never inline it.\n    #[inline(never)]\n    #[instrument(level = \"debug\", skip(self, pending_obligation))]\n    fn process_obligation(\n        &mut self,\n        pending_obligation: &mut PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx>,\n    ) -> ProcessResult<PendingPredicateObligation<'tcx>, FulfillmentErrorCode<'tcx>> {\n        pending_obligation.stalled_on.truncate(0);\n\n        let obligation = &mut pending_obligation.obligation;\n\n        debug!(?obligation, \"pre-resolve\");\n\n        if obligation.predicate.has_non_region_infer() {\n            obligation.predicate = self.selcx.infcx.resolve_vars_if_possible(obligation.predicate);\n        }\n\n        let obligation = &pending_obligation.obligation;\n\n        let infcx = self.selcx.infcx;\n\n        if sizedness_fast_path(infcx.tcx, obligation.predicate, obligation.param_env) {\n            return ProcessResult::Changed(thin_vec![]);\n        }\n\n        if obligation.predicate.has_aliases() {\n            let mut obligations = PredicateObligations::new();\n            let predicate = normalize_with_depth_to(\n                &mut self.selcx,\n                obligation.param_env,\n                obligation.cause.clone(),\n                obligation.recursion_depth + 1,\n                obligation.predicate,\n                &mut obligations,\n            );\n            if predicate != obligation.predicate {\n                obligations.push(obligation.with(infcx.tcx, predicate));\n                return ProcessResult::Changed(mk_pending(obligation, obligations));\n            }\n        }\n        let binder = obligation.predicate.kind();\n        match binder.no_bound_vars() {\n            None => match binder.skip_binder() {\n                // Evaluation will discard candidates using the leak check.\n                // This means we need to pass it the bound version of our\n                // predicate.\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::Trait(trait_ref)) => {\n                    let trait_obligation = obligation.with(infcx.tcx, binder.rebind(trait_ref));\n\n                    self.process_trait_obligation(\n                        obligation,\n                        trait_obligation,\n                        &mut pending_obligation.stalled_on,\n                    )\n                }\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::Projection(data)) => {\n                    let project_obligation = obligation.with(infcx.tcx, binder.rebind(data));\n\n                    self.process_projection_obligation(\n                        obligation,\n                        project_obligation,\n                        &mut pending_obligation.stalled_on,\n                    )\n                }\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::RegionOutlives(_))\n                | ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::TypeOutlives(_))\n                | ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::ConstArgHasType(..))\n                | ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::WellFormed(_))\n                | ty::PredicateKind::DynCompatible(_)\n                | ty::PredicateKind::Subtype(_)\n                | ty::PredicateKind::Coerce(_)\n                | ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::ConstEvaluatable(..))\n                | ty::PredicateKind::ConstEquate(..)\n                // FIXME(const_trait_impl): We may need to do this using the higher-ranked\n                // pred instead of just instantiating it with placeholders b/c of\n                // higher-ranked implied bound issues in the old solver.\n                | ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::HostEffect(..)) => {\n                    let pred = ty::Binder::dummy(infcx.enter_forall_and_leak_universe(binder));\n                    let mut obligations = PredicateObligations::with_capacity(1);\n                    obligations.push(obligation.with(infcx.tcx, pred));\n\n                    ProcessResult::Changed(mk_pending(obligation, obligations))\n                }\n                ty::PredicateKind::Ambiguous => ProcessResult::Unchanged,\n                ty::PredicateKind::NormalizesTo(..) => {\n                    bug!(\"NormalizesTo is only used by the new solver\")\n                }\n                ty::PredicateKind::AliasRelate(..) => {\n                    bug!(\"AliasRelate is only used by the new solver\")\n                }\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::UnstableFeature(_)) => {\n                   unreachable!(\"unexpected higher ranked `UnstableFeature` goal\")\n                }\n            },\n            Some(pred) => match pred {\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::Trait(data)) => {\n                    let trait_obligation = obligation.with(infcx.tcx, Binder::dummy(data));\n\n                    self.process_trait_obligation(\n                        obligation,\n                        trait_obligation,\n                        &mut pending_obligation.stalled_on,\n                    )\n                }\n\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::HostEffect(data)) => {\n                    let host_obligation = obligation.with(infcx.tcx, data);\n\n                    self.process_host_obligation(\n                        obligation,\n                        host_obligation,\n                        &mut pending_obligation.stalled_on,\n                    )\n                }\n\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::RegionOutlives(data)) => {\n                    if infcx.considering_regions {\n                        infcx.register_region_outlives_constraint(data, &obligation.cause);\n                    }\n\n                    ProcessResult::Changed(Default::default())\n                }\n\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::TypeOutlives(ty::OutlivesPredicate(\n                    t_a,\n                    r_b,\n                ))) => {\n                    if infcx.considering_regions {\n                        infcx.register_type_outlives_constraint(t_a, r_b, &obligation.cause);\n                    }\n                    ProcessResult::Changed(Default::default())\n                }\n\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::Projection(ref data)) => {\n                    let project_obligation = obligation.with(infcx.tcx, Binder::dummy(*data));\n\n                    self.process_projection_obligation(\n                        obligation,\n                        project_obligation,\n                        &mut pending_obligation.stalled_on,\n                    )\n                }\n\n                ty::PredicateKind::DynCompatible(trait_def_id) => {\n                    if !self.selcx.tcx().is_dyn_compatible(trait_def_id) {\n                        ProcessResult::Error(FulfillmentErrorCode::Select(\n                            SelectionError::Unimplemented,\n                        ))\n                    } else {\n                        ProcessResult::Changed(Default::default())\n                    }\n                }\n\n                ty::PredicateKind::Ambiguous => ProcessResult::Unchanged,\n                ty::PredicateKind::NormalizesTo(..) => {\n                    bug!(\"NormalizesTo is only used by the new solver\")\n                }\n                ty::PredicateKind::AliasRelate(..) => {\n                    bug!(\"AliasRelate is only used by the new solver\")\n                }\n                // Compute `ConstArgHasType` above the overflow check below.\n                // This is because this is not ever a useful obligation to report\n                // as the cause of an overflow.\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::ConstArgHasType(ct, ty)) => {\n                    let ct = infcx.shallow_resolve_const(ct);\n                    let ct_ty = match ct.kind() {\n                        ty::ConstKind::Infer(var) => {\n                            let var = match var {\n                                ty::InferConst::Var(vid) => TyOrConstInferVar::Const(vid),\n                                ty::InferConst::Fresh(_) => {\n                                    bug!(\"encountered fresh const in fulfill\")\n                                }\n                            };\n                            pending_obligation.stalled_on.clear();\n                            pending_obligation.stalled_on.extend([var]);\n                            return ProcessResult::Unchanged;\n                        }\n                        ty::ConstKind::Error(_) => {\n                            return ProcessResult::Changed(PendingPredicateObligations::new());\n                        }\n                        ty::ConstKind::Value(cv) => cv.ty,\n                        ty::ConstKind::Unevaluated(uv) => {\n                            infcx.tcx.type_of(uv.def).instantiate(infcx.tcx, uv.args)\n                        }\n                        // FIXME(generic_const_exprs): we should construct an alias like\n                        // `<lhs_ty as Add<rhs_ty>>::Output` when this is an `Expr` representing\n                        // `lhs + rhs`.\n                        ty::ConstKind::Expr(_) => {\n                            return ProcessResult::Changed(mk_pending(\n                                obligation,\n                                PredicateObligations::new(),\n                            ));\n                        }\n                        ty::ConstKind::Placeholder(_) => {\n                            bug!(\"placeholder const {:?} in old solver\", ct)\n                        }\n                        ty::ConstKind::Bound(_, _) => bug!(\"escaping bound vars in {:?}\", ct),\n                        ty::ConstKind::Param(param_ct) => {\n                            param_ct.find_const_ty_from_env(obligation.param_env)\n                        }\n                    };\n\n                    match infcx.at(&obligation.cause, obligation.param_env).eq(\n                        // Only really exercised by generic_const_exprs\n                        DefineOpaqueTypes::Yes,\n                        ct_ty,\n                        ty,\n                    ) {\n                        Ok(inf_ok) => ProcessResult::Changed(mk_pending(\n                            obligation,\n                            inf_ok.into_obligations(),\n                        )),\n                        Err(_) => ProcessResult::Error(FulfillmentErrorCode::Select(\n                            SelectionError::ConstArgHasWrongType { ct, ct_ty, expected_ty: ty },\n                        )),\n                    }\n                }\n\n                // General case overflow check. Allow `process_trait_obligation`\n                // and `process_projection_obligation` to handle checking for\n                // the recursion limit themselves. Also don't check some\n                // predicate kinds that don't give further obligations.\n                _ if !self\n                    .selcx\n                    .tcx()\n                    .recursion_limit()\n                    .value_within_limit(obligation.recursion_depth) =>\n                {\n                    self.selcx.infcx.err_ctxt().report_overflow_obligation(&obligation, false);\n                }\n\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::WellFormed(term)) => {\n                    if term.is_trivially_wf(self.selcx.tcx()) {\n                        return ProcessResult::Changed(thin_vec![]);\n                    }\n\n                    match wf::obligations(\n                        self.selcx.infcx,\n                        obligation.param_env,\n                        obligation.cause.body_id,\n                        obligation.recursion_depth + 1,\n                        term,\n                        obligation.cause.span,\n                    ) {\n                        None => {\n                            pending_obligation.stalled_on =\n                                vec![TyOrConstInferVar::maybe_from_term(term).unwrap()];\n                            ProcessResult::Unchanged\n                        }\n                        Some(os) => ProcessResult::Changed(mk_pending(obligation, os)),\n                    }\n                }\n\n                ty::PredicateKind::Subtype(subtype) => {\n                    match self.selcx.infcx.subtype_predicate(\n                        &obligation.cause,\n                        obligation.param_env,\n                        Binder::dummy(subtype),\n                    ) {\n                        Err((a, b)) => {\n                            // None means that both are unresolved.\n                            pending_obligation.stalled_on =\n                                vec![TyOrConstInferVar::Ty(a), TyOrConstInferVar::Ty(b)];\n                            ProcessResult::Unchanged\n                        }\n                        Ok(Ok(ok)) => {\n                            ProcessResult::Changed(mk_pending(obligation, ok.obligations))\n                        }\n                        Ok(Err(err)) => {\n                            let expected_found = if subtype.a_is_expected {\n                                ExpectedFound::new(subtype.a, subtype.b)\n                            } else {\n                                ExpectedFound::new(subtype.b, subtype.a)\n                            };\n                            ProcessResult::Error(FulfillmentErrorCode::Subtype(expected_found, err))\n                        }\n                    }\n                }\n\n                ty::PredicateKind::Coerce(coerce) => {\n                    match self.selcx.infcx.coerce_predicate(\n                        &obligation.cause,\n                        obligation.param_env,\n                        Binder::dummy(coerce),\n                    ) {\n                        Err((a, b)) => {\n                            // None means that both are unresolved.\n                            pending_obligation.stalled_on =\n                                vec![TyOrConstInferVar::Ty(a), TyOrConstInferVar::Ty(b)];\n                            ProcessResult::Unchanged\n                        }\n                        Ok(Ok(ok)) => {\n                            ProcessResult::Changed(mk_pending(obligation, ok.obligations))\n                        }\n                        Ok(Err(err)) => {\n                            let expected_found = ExpectedFound::new(coerce.b, coerce.a);\n                            ProcessResult::Error(FulfillmentErrorCode::Subtype(expected_found, err))\n                        }\n                    }\n                }\n\n                ty::PredicateKind::Clause(ty::ClauseKind::ConstEvaluatable(uv)) => {\n                    match const_evaluatable::is_const_evaluatable(\n                        self.selcx.infcx,\n                        uv,\n                        obligation.param_env,\n                        obligation.cause.span,\n                    ) {\n                        Ok(()) => ProcessResult::Changed(Default::default()),\n                        Err(NotConstEvaluatable::MentionsInfer) => {\n                            pending_obligation.stalled_on.clear();\n                            pending_obligation.stalled_on.extend(\n                                uv.walk().filter_map(TyOrConstInferVar::maybe_from_generic_arg),\n                            );\n                            ProcessResult::Unchanged\n                        }\n                        Err(\n                            e @ NotConstEvaluatable::MentionsParam\n                            | e @ NotConstEvaluatable::Error(_),\n                        ) => ProcessResult::Error(FulfillmentErrorCode::Select(\n                            SelectionError::NotConstEvaluatable(e),\n                        )),\n                    }\n                }\n\n                ty::PredicateKind::ConstEquate(c1, c2) => {\n                    let tcx = self.selcx.tcx();\n                    assert!(\n                        tcx.features().generic_const_exprs(),\n                        \"`ConstEquate` without a feature gate: {c1:?} {c2:?}\",\n                    );\n                    // FIXME: we probably should only try to unify abstract constants\n                    // if the constants depend on generic parameters.\n                    //\n                    // Let's just see where this breaks :shrug:\n                    {\n                        let c1 = tcx.expand_abstract_consts(c1);\n                        let c2 = tcx.expand_abstract_consts(c2);\n                        debug!(\"equating consts:\\nc1= {:?}\\nc2= {:?}\", c1, c2);\n\n                        use rustc_hir::def::DefKind;\n                        match (c1.kind(), c2.kind()) {\n                            (ty::ConstKind::Unevaluated(a), ty::ConstKind::Unevaluated(b))\n                                if a.def == b.def && tcx.def_kind(a.def) == DefKind::AssocConst =>\n                            {\n                                if let Ok(new_obligations) = infcx\n                                    .at(&obligation.cause, obligation.param_env)\n                                    // Can define opaque types as this is only reachable with\n                                    // `generic_const_exprs`\n                                    .eq(\n                                        DefineOpaqueTypes::Yes,\n                                        ty::AliasTerm::from(a),\n                                        ty::AliasTerm::from(b),\n                                    )\n                                {\n                                    return ProcessResult::Changed(mk_pending(\n                                        obligation,\n                                        new_obligations.into_obligations(),\n                                    ));\n                                }\n                            }\n                            (_, ty::ConstKind::Unevaluated(_))\n                            | (ty::ConstKind::Unevaluated(_), _) => (),\n                            (_, _) => {\n                                if let with\n                                    // `generic_const_exprs`\n                                    .eq(DefineOpaqueTypes::Yes, c1, c2)\n                                {\n                    ));\n                                }\n                            }\n                        }\n                    }\n\n                    let stalled_on = &mut pending_obligation.stalled_on;\n\n                    let mut evaluate = |c: Const<'tcx>| {\n                        if let ty::ConstKind::Unevaluated(unevaluated) = c.kind() {\n         ) {\n                                Ok(val) => Ok(val),\n                                e @ Err(EvaluateConstErr::HasGenericsOrInfers) => {\n                                    stalled_on.extend(\n                                        unevaluated\n                                            .args\n                                            .iter()\n                                            .filter_map(TyOrConstInferVar::maybe_from_generic_arg),\n                                    );\n                                    e\n                                }\n                     ) => e,\n                            }\n                        } else {\n                            Ok(c)\n                        }\n                    };\n\n
